and  music  and  everything.   The  parents  met  before  church  for  an 
hour  and  discussed  issues.   It  was  very  exciting. 


616 


Then  Dr.  Ivar  Hellstrom,  who  was  the  head  of  the  Sunday 
School,  retired  and  they  brought  in  this  Methodist  minister  who 
decided  that  this  kind  of  Sunday  School  was  inappropriate,  and 
too  liberal,  and  they  were  going  to  make  it  a  neo-orthodox 
Sunday  School.   (Union  Theological  Seminary  was  big  on  neo- 
orthodoxy  at  that  time,  and  it  was  right  across  the  street.) 
This  minister  did  everything  in  a  very  backhanded  way  to  make 
this  come  about  and  was  also  dishonest  about  it. 

So  Charlie  and  I  resigned  from  Riverside  Church.   Nobody 
had  ever  done  that  in  the  history  of  Riverside.   I  mean,  if  you 
were  a  deacon  and  you  could  wear  those  striped  pants  and  tails 
which  you  got  out  of  a  box- -they  all  had  to  wear  them,  it  was 
sort  of  the  Rockefeller  stamp  on  the  church- -who  would  ever 
resign? 

There  was  a  distinguished  theologian,  now  dead,  named  John 
Bennett,  who  was  professor  of  applied  Christianity  at  Union, 
and  they  had  one  child.   They  wished  to  change  the  whole  Sunday 
School  for  that  child  to  be  brought  up  in  a  sort  of  neo- 
orthodox  kind  of  Sunday  School.   So  there  were  political  and 
religious  issues.   Charlie  went  to  see  John  Bennett,  who  called 
him  every  name  in  the  book.   I  remember  sitting  outside- -we 
were  going  to  dinner- -and  he  came  out  and  he  said,  "We  have  to 
leave  this  church." 

Well,  we  had  so  many  friends  there  and  had  been  prominent, 
so  it  was  difficult.   Again,  it  was  like  when  we  left  MIT.   The 
students  were  angry  at  the  choice  they  made  for  the  president 
so  they  painted  the  dome  of  MIT's  large  building  orange  because 
MIT  had  chosen  Howard  Johnson.   You  know  Howard  Johnson 
restaurants  had  those  little  orange-roofed  houses?   Well,  there 
was  a  kind  of  rebellion  at  Riverside,  and  our  friends  were 
angry.   And,  guess  what?  This  man  was  fired.   [pause] 

Riess:      Howard  Johnson? 

F.  Townes:   No,  no,  not  Howard  Johnson  but  the  minister  at  Riverside.   It 
was  finally  discovered  that  he  had  done  all  of  these  sleazy 
things . 

And  Charlie  was  also  in  liaison  with  the  members  of  the 
church  on  campus.  I'll  never  forget  how  we  had  a  dinner  for 
Dr.  Hellstrom  who  was  retiring  as  head  of  the  Sunday  School, 
and  we  invited  this  new  man.  We  really  went  out  of  our  way  to 
meet  him  and  to  bring  him  in.  And  he  just  forgot  to  come  to 
dinner.  I  remember  sitting  there  for  an  hour  waiting,  and  I 
finally  called  his  house,  "Oh,  I'm  sorry.  I  forgot."  So  we 


617 

fed  the  grand  old  man  a  cold  dinner.   That  was  such  a  demeaning 
experience . 

We  were  being  called  all  kinds  of  names  at  Riverside,  too 
liberal,  etc.   We  actually  went  up  to  a  not-so-liberal 
Presbyterian  church  in  Riverdale.   You  see,  we  have  never  been 
linked  to  a  particular  denomination.   We've  been  Lutherans  in 
Cambridge,  and  we've  been  Presbyterians,  and  we've  been  pretty 
much  everything  in  the  non- fundamental  protestant  spectrum.   So 
those  were  times  when  I  would  say  that  principle- -things  we 
believed  in  very  strongly --tested  us. 

Riess:      But  you  referred  to  truth  and  the  kinds  of  struggles  you've  had 
over  truth.   Tell  me  more  about  what  truth  really  means  to 
Charlie.   I'm  not  sure  what  you  are  saying.   Because  I  can 
understand  that  there  is  much  in  life  that  is  inexact  and 
unprovable  and  knowable . 

F.  Townes:   I  wish  I  could  put  it  into  words.   I've  thought  about  it  often, 
but  it--. 

The  way  we've  had  growth  and  pain  in  our  marriage  is  just 
over  that  issue.   For  example,  arguments  we  might  have  in  which 
I  would  fight  back- -I  no  longer  do  this  so  much,  but  usually  I 
would  argue --but  Charlie's  legal  mind  or  whatever  the  logic  of 
it  was  got  us  into,  "You  said  this,  and  "You  said  that."   "How 
can  you  prove  it?"   "All  right.   Well,  how  can  you  prove  it?" 
But  I  couldn't,  and  he  could.   So  there  you  are. 

It  was  a  kind  of  bridge  across  which  we  have  moved,  and  I 
have  somehow  been  the  one  who  has  pushed  that  area  in  our 
lives.   But  it  is  very  strenuous  to  deal  with  a  man  who  has  a 
kind  of --whose  father  was  a  lawyer,  and  who  is  a  scientist  and 
can  catch  you  coming  and  going,  and  say  you  said  this  and  you 
said  that.   And  you  don't  quite  understand,  so  you  end  up 
sounding  negative. 

And  it's  true  that  I  am  a  little  thick  sometimes,  and  a 
little  free  with  the  truth.   It's  been  my  shadow  side.   My 
mother  also  was  that  way,  and  when  she  came  to  visit  us  it  was 
always  so  difficult,  because  Mother  would  sit  there  and  babble 
on.   "What  are  facts  to  a  raconteur?"  was  her  approach.   She 
was  a  charming  woman,  and  she  wanted  to  ask  Charlie  all  of  the 
right  questions,  and  it  was  so  painful,  because  these  two 
people  were  as  far  apart  as  they  could  possibly  be.   He  wanted 
to  talk  very  specifically,  and  she  was  flailing  around  with  her 
facts.   Mother  was  a  very  intelligent  woman,  and  it  wasn't 
because  she  wasn't  intelligent,  but  she  was  not  an  expert  in 
science.   She  had  been  trained  that  you  bring  the  other  person 


618 


out  by  asking  about  themselves  and  their  work.   Oh,  it  was 
difficult. 

Riess:      That's  interesting.   I  can  picture  that.   I  asked  him  whether 
in  a  situation  where  he  disagrees  with  someone,  where,  in  fact, 
the  other  person  stands  for  things  that  are  anathema  to  him,  he 
can  learn  from  that  person.   Can  what  that  person  has  to  offer 
be  of  value?  And  I  begin  to  wonder  from  what  you  are  saying, 
whether-- 

F.  Townes:   It  would  be  hard  for  him. 

Riess:      --it  doesn't  just  close  him  off  from  certain  kinds  of 
information? 

F.  Townes:   Oh!   No.   I  think  it's  hard  for  him,  however,  because  when  he's 
made  up  his  mind,  he's  made  up.  his  mind.   There  isn't  much 
flexibility.   I  don't  think  I've  ever  said  that  before,  but  I 
think  that's  probably  true. 

You  have  to  realize  that,  of  course,  I'm  seeing  him 
through  my  glass  darkly,  through  who  I  am,  so  that  I  would 
judge  him  more  severely  than  maybe  a  scientist.   Scientists  are 
used  to  going  head  on,  and  each  with  their  own  idea,  and  I 
don't  think  that  for  them  it  is  such  a  cosmic  kind  of  issue. 
But  between  a  humanist  and  a  scientist  there  would  be- -their 
ways  of  seeing  things  and  his  could  be  different.   Who  would 
give  in? 

Certainly  this  whole  issue  of  homosexuality  has  been  very 
tender.   We  are  having  discussions  at  the  church  about  sexual 
ethics  right  now  which  he  has  initiated,  and  we're  going  to 
have  a  meeting  here  in  this  house  to  talk  about  them.   But 
Charlie  and  I  are  able  to  see  each  other's  points  of  view  now. 
You  know,  people  do  grow,  just  the  way  I've  grown  in  my  ability 
to  see  things  in  a  less  anecdotal  and  more  realistic  way.   I 
think  we've  grown  together  more.   We  never  will  be  exactly 
alike,  but  I  think  I've  grown  in  areas  where  he's  strong,  and  I 
think  he  has  in  areas  where  I'm  strong. 


The  Shadow  Side  of  the  Social  Life 


F.  Townes:  I  was  interested  that  you  said  [in  your  letter]  he  was  socially 
adept.  I  hadn't,  at  least  certainly  in  the  early  days,  thought 
of  him  that  way.  And  that  people  liked  to  have  him  at  parties. 
Well,  it's  partially  because  he's  kind  of  a  VIP,  and  he  has  had 


619 


Riess: 


F.  Townes: 


Riess  : 


F.  Townes: 


an  interesting  life,  and  does  exciting  things,  and  he  does  talk 
well.   So  I  think  he  has  graced  many  dinner  parties  for  that 
reason.   But  most  of  the  entertaining  I  have  done  has  been  with 
scientists.   Except  when  at  MIT  I  had  those  weekly  dinner 
parties  when  we  would  have  a  couple  from  Harvard  and  one  from 
MIT,  and  then  maybe  a  professor  we  were  courting.   Small  dinner 
parties  of  eight  to  ten  people,  an  intermingling,  trying  to 
bring  Harvard  and  MIT  together,  and  we  would  often  have 
humanists. 

Houston  Smith,  the  distinguished  humanist  who  was  head  of 
the  philosophy  department  at  MIT,  who  came  out  here  when  he 
retired,  and  who  is  a  sort  of  semi -Buddhist,  and  who  has 
written  books  on  world  religions--!  got  him  to  speak  at  the 
church.   He  helped  us  to  do  a  whole  series  on  Buddhism  and 
Hinduism  and  Islam  and  other  world  religions  in  our  Second  Hour 
after  worship.   He  was  a  great -admirer  of  Charlie's.   Houston 
is  rather  a  mystic  person. 

So  Charlie  has  had  friends  and  certainly  brought  people  to 
MIT  who  weren't  scientists.  One  good  friend  was  the  artist  who 
did  that  painting,  Gyorgy  Kepes,  and  also  his  wife.   Kay 
[Stratton]  and  I  were  talking  about  setting  up  a  center  where 
there  would  be  an  artist  in  residence,  where  students  could 
come  and  see  a  person  doing  art.   Kepes  was  at  that  center. 
Charlie  has  a  real  appreciation  of  the  arts.   But  I  don't  think 
he  has  as  much  understanding  as  I  have.   It  was  not  in  his 
background.   Music,  but  not  art. 

I  could  see  that  that  might  be  hard.   You  might  end  up  feeling 
that  the  man  was  simply  tolerating  an  area,  but  essentially  not 
respecting  it. 

No,  I  think  he  respected  it.   I  don't  think  he  respects  a  lot 
of  modern  art,  particularly,  but  he's  not  unique  in  that.   His 
own  [parents']  home  had  a  leather  couch  and  a  mirror  over  the 
mantelpiece.   It  was  all  perfectly  all  right,  but  it  was  in  my 
estimation  not  an  artistic  ambiance. 

Talking  about  Charlie's  "social  life,"  if  he  were  president  of 
MIT  he'd  have  to  have  come  to  terms  with  lots  and  lots  of 
dinner  parties. 

There  again,  I  would  say  it  was  a  typical  Charlie  dilemma. 
When  you  realize  that  he  really  has  given  up  quite  a  lot  of  his 
life  to  working  on  arms  control  and  disarmament,  and  we  spent 
two  years  in  Washington  around  these  issues,  when  he  didn't  do 
any  science--.   Then  MIT  gave  him  minimal  time  for  science 
because  of  his  provost  responsibilities. 


620 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


So  how  do  you  contribute  to  the  world?  And  if  you  are 
contributing  as  an  administrator,  that  is  good.   But  in  terms 
of  his  own  personality  and  his  own  talents,  it  would  not  have 
been  very  satisfying  to  have  been  president  of  MIT.   It  would 
have  been  difficult  for  him.   It  might  have  been  my  dish  of 
tea,  not  his  dish  of  tea. 

Now,  I'm  not  sure  it  would  have  been  my  dish  of  tea  in  the 
long  run  either,  because  that's  a  very  difficult  job.   You  have 
to  be  living  on  a  kind  of  superficial  level  all  the  time.   And 
Charlie  does  not  like  small  talk,  it  is  wasting  time,  and  that 
job  would  have  just  been  full  of  that  kind  of  thing. 

Already,  as  provost's  wife,  my  children  would  say, 
"Mother,  you  don't  know  who  your  real  friends  are.   Everybody's 
nice  to  you  because  their  husband  might  be  sacked  for  somebody 
else."   Charlie  was  in  the  position  of  hiring  and  firing 
professors;  that  was  part  of  his  job. 

Why  were  your  daughters  saying  this? 

Well,  they  were  just  saying,  "Yes,  Mom,  you  like  Suzy  and  she's 
so  nice  to  you.   How  do  you  know  whether  she  really  likes  you, 
or  whether  it's  because  she's  concerned  about  her  husband's 
job?" 


Oh  dear. 

Well,  that  happens  to  people  in  high  places, 
don't  know  half  the  time  who--. 


It's  painful.   I 


I  went  last  night  for  the  first  time  in  a  couple  of  years 
to  an  opening  at  the  Oakland  Museum,  the  opening  for  the  Indian 
exhibition.   I  was  overwhelmed  by  people  being  nice  to  me 
purely,  I  knew,  because  they  want  me  to  give  money  again,  which 
I  have  stopped  giving.   I  was  invited  because  I  got  to  be  a 
"colleague,"  or  whatever  the  top  rank  is,  because  I  gave,  from 
my  own  money,  one  thousand  dollars  every  year,  and  I  finally 
got  to  that  top  level.   So  I  am  on  that  list  that  gets  invited, 
and  I  am  being  pushed,  pushed,  pushed  by  letters  all  the  time. 
Tom  Frye  sidled  up  to  me  and  gave  me  the  sweet  talk,  and  I  just 
realized  how  much  I  disliked  that. 

I  went  and  sat  at  the  table  with  Dolly  Dreiman  and  Jim 
Uren  and  a  few  of  my  old  buddies  from  [the]  natural  science 
[division  of  the  museum]  because  I  couldn't  stand  it.   Therese 
[Heyman]  was  there,  and  sort  of  was  wanting  me  to  sit  with  her, 
and  I  just  went  zooming  by  and  went  over  there  where  we  could 
sort  of  giggle  on  our  own  about  olden  days.   We  were  also 


621 


talking  about  the  new  president  [of  the  Oakland  Museum 
Foundation].   "Isn't  he  a  bore,  how  can  he  raise  any  money?" 
And  Kay  Winer  came  up  and  said,  "We  haven't  seen  you  in  such  a 
long  time ! " 

I  just  knew  that  that's  the  kind  of  thing  that  I  would  be 
subjected  to  at  MIT.   And  I'm  really  too  ebullient  a  person  to 
take  that  job  because  sure  enough,  I  would  say  something 
indiscreet  or  I  would  say  too  much,  or  I  would--!  don't  know. 
So  I  think  it  was  God's  grace  that  we  didn't  stay  at  MIT! 
[laughter] 

Charlie  doesn't  suffer  fools  unnecessarily,  and  you  asked 
if  I  helped  him--.   One  of  the  things  is  he  always  likes  to 
leave  the  party  first.   He's  come,  he's  done  his  duty- -unless 
he  gets  into  a  corner  with  some  like-minded  person  and  they're 
having  a  great  talk.   He  grabs  my  arm  and  expects  to  leave 
immediately.   I  keep  saying,  "But  we  have  to  say  thank-you  to 
the  hosts."   So  I  have  to  sort  of  hold  on  to  him  before  he 
leaves,  to  make  it  more  gracious.   I  think  he's  learned  to  do 
that  better  now. 


"A  Little  Leeway"  for  Scientists 


Riess:      But  don't  you  think  that  great  scientists  are  given  a  lot  of 
leeway  in  life? 

F.  Townes:   Oh,  well  maybe  they  are,  but  why  should  they  be?  They're  just 
people. 

I  am,  right  now- -I  have  been  for  about  six  or  eight 
months- -going  to  a  spiritual  advisor  at  the  church,  who  is  our 
associate  minister,  because  I  had  become  so  disenchanted  with 
our  minister  for  whom  I  had  worked  for  seven  years,  who  left 
about  two  months  ago,  that  I  was  on  the  verge  of  resigning  from 
my  job  at  the  church,  which  was  a  volunteer  job,  but  a 
responsible  one  with  adult  education.   I  had  been  so  tired  of 
being  used:  "Fran  will  do  this  and  that  and  the  other." 

An  example  is  a  note  in  my  box  late  on  a  Wednesday 
afternoon:  "I  forgot  to  tell  you.   I  will  be  away  tomorrow, 
will  you  lead  the  Bible  class?  If  you  can't,  will  you  call  the 
members  to  tell  them  that  it  won't  be  held?"   So  I  was  caught 
either  way:  I  didn't  have  a  chance  to  prepare  or  I  had  to  spend 
time  calling  a  whole  lot  of  people.   I  mean,  I  had  this  kind  of 
experience  constantly.   He  was  a  lousy  manager  and 
administrator,  and  he's  gone  to  the  Northeast,  to  a  huge 


622 


church.   He  and  I  had  many  objectives  in  common,  but  he  took  me 
for  granted.  Volunteering  was  being  utterly  flexible  for  him. 

So  I  was  having  a  lot  of  pain  in  that,  and  I  was  working 
that  through  with  Foster,  the  associate  minister.   The  day  that 
the  senior  minister  came  in  and  threw  down  his  letter  and  said, 
"I'm  leaving,"  I  was  Just  going  to  resign  that  very  day.   It 
was  quite  a  cosmic  moment  for  me ,  because  I  had  been  very 
active  in  the  church  and  a  leader.   I  had  plenty  else  to  do 
with  the  homeless  work,  but  it  was  a  big  decision.   Then  he 
said,  "Well,  of  course,  you'll  all  have  to  stay  on  another  year 
until  we  get  another  minister."   So  I'm  stuck  for  a  while 
still. 

I  never  did  quite  confront  him,  but  I  worked  through  a  lot 
of  that  just  recently.   Then,  when  he  left,  I  was  asked  to  make 
a  little  speech  at  a  farewell  party  for  him  at  the  church,  and 
I  had  to  get  up  and  say  all  of  the  marvelous  things  that  he  had 
done  about  the  homeless.   Well,  !  had  done  most  of  those 
wonderful  things,  and  I  had  not  been  recognized  for  them.   I 
had  never  been  asked  to  speak  about  homelessness  before. 

Anyway,  I  had  to  get  up  and  try  to--.   And  I  could  have 
refused.   I  decided  that  it  would  be  a  very  good  test  of  my  own 
kind  of  forgiveness  and  clarity  on  it  to  do  it.   I  sure  prayed 
a  lot  about  it.   I  went  on  a  retreat  of  a.  class  in  spirituality 
that  Foster  gave  just  before  this.   It  was  my  birthday,  so  the 
rest  of  the  people  said,  "You  can  have  the  one  single  room," 
and  I  went  up  and  thought  a  lot  about  it.   I  also  wanted  to  do 
it  in  a  relaxed  way  without  reading  it. 

I  was  able  to  find  quite  a  few  things  that  he  had  actually 
done.   I  mean,  he  had  had  an  idea  or  whatever.   He  had  come 
with  this  open  idea  about  social  change.  Apparently  my  speech 
went  very  well.   It  also  gave  me  a  chance,  at  last,  to  say  that 
this  agency  that  I  had  started  almost  four  years  ago  is  in 
danger  of  dying  because  of  lack  of  money,  like  many  social 
agencies,  and  if  it  dies  it  would  have  to  be  reinvented.   I  was 
able  to  say  a  few  things  that  were  significant  to  me,  and  I  had 
probably  the  biggest  audience  that  I'll  ever  have.   And  I  said 
it  with  grace  and  so  forth. 

So--.   I'm  not  quite  clear  what  I'm  saying  except  that 
that  had  been  a  threshold  for  me.   And  I  have  also  been 
journaling  daily,  very  profoundly,  with  dreams  and  so  forth, 
these  last  two  months.   It's  been  a  very  creative  time  for  me. 
Knowing  who  I  am  and  what  my  calling  is  and  knowing  who  I  am 
separate  from  Charlie,  yes,  it's  been  a  very  creative  time. 


623 


Riess:      It's  funny,  I've  forgotten  completely  what  the  Charlie  question 
was. 

F.  Townes:   Yes,  I  know.   That's  what  I  was  afraid  of,  my  rambling  on  about 
myself.   I  don't  know  where  we  went  off. 


Sartorial  Asides## 


Riess:      All  right,  so  the  question  was  about  the  leeway  scientists  are 
given. 

F.  Townes:   All  right,  they  are  allowed  to  be  a  little  quirky  in  their 

dress.   But  I  was  brought  up  with  a  family  where  people  weren't 
allowed  to  be  quirky  in  their  dress.   My  brothers  were  all  very 
handsome  and  beautifully  built,  and  my  father  was  smaller  than 
me,  but  well-built.   They  all  always  looked  wonderful  in 
clothes.   Charles,  and  that  is  typically  scientist,  didn't  give 
a  darn  about  his  appearance.   He  had  better  things  to  think 
about.   I  would  be  almost  embarrassed,  sometimes.   I  almost 
felt  that  I  would  be  judged  if  he  appeared  badly  put  together, 
a  crumpled  shirt  or  whatever.   And  yet,  to  kind  of  suggest  that 
he  might  wear  another  shirt,  or  tie,  well--. 

Now,  I'm  sure  my  influence  has  moved  him  a  bit.   I  had  a 
certain  pride  in  the  fact  that  he  should  look  as  well  as  he 
could,  and  it  was  harder  for  him  because  he  was  not  a  clothes 
horse.   My  brothers  could  put  on  anything  and  look  well. 
Charlie  couldn't  do  that,  so  I  felt  it  was  more  important  for 
him  to  care,  rather  than  less  so.   Through  the  years  I've 
thought,  "Oh  Goodness,  Charlie,  you  look  like  you  slept  in  your 
clothes!"  When  we  were  going  to  France,  to  meet  the  President 
of  France,  he  didn't  have  a  dark  suit!   He  just  didn't.   He's 
even  refused  to  get  a  dark  blue  blazer  for  example. 

He  agreed  to  go  to  George  Good's,  and  dear  old  George 
Good's  had  a  suit  on  sale,  you  wouldn't  believe  it,  at  around 
one  hundred  and  seventy-five  dollars,  and  it  fit  him;  we  got 
it,  and  they  fixed  the  trousers.   He  really  looked  quite 
handsome  in  it.   Then  he  went  off  to  India,  and  he  carried  it 
all  the  way  through  India  and  came  back  through  Zurich. 

He  got  to  Paris  a  day  before  I  did,  and  they  had  lost  his 
luggage  in  Zurich,  with  his  dark  suit  in  it.   When  I  arrived, 
he  had  been  to  Galeries  Lafayette  to  get  another  dark  suit, 
[laughter]   And  of  course  the  luggage  arrived  and  he  ended  up 
with  two  dark  suits.   So  now  he'll  have  them  the  rest  of  his 


624 


life.   But  it  was  just--.   And  he  looked  very  nice  for  the 
occasion. 

That  was  finally  ironed  out.   I  was  wanting  him  to  look 
his  best.   I  try  to  look  well.   I  don't  spend  much  money  on 
clothes,  but  I  have  a  knack  of  putting  things  together.   All  of 
these  things  I  have  on  today  are  from  White  Elephant  sales  at 
the  Oakland  Museum,  or  from  earlier  periods  of  my  life.   This 
is  terribly  old,  this  one  I  bought  at  the  last  White  Elephant 
sale  for  five  dollars.   I  know  how  to  mix  and  match. 

Riess:      [laughing]   You  mean  that's  my  old  gray  sweater  you  are 
wearing? 

F.  Townes:   Probably,  yes.   It  probably  is.   [laughter]   And  you  know,  1  do 
know  how  to  get  myself  together,  and  I  think  Charlie,  in  a 
sense,  appreciates  that.   He  gives  me  lots  of  interesting  semi 
precious  and  costume  jewelry,  especially  earrings- -new  ones 
more  recently  when  I  had  my  ears  pierced.   They  help  me  to  vary 
my  old  clothes.   Get  him  to  tell  you  about  the  beautiful  pin  he 
had  designed  for  me  for  our  twentieth  wedding  anniversary  when 
we  were  in  Washington.   It  has  a  maser  ruby  and  it  has 
interesting  symbolism. 

Oh,  let  me  tell  you  another  sartorial  thing  that  is  really 
interesting.   I  don't  think  my  clothes  matter  to  him  very  much. 
I'm  sure  if  I  came  down  looking  like  I  slept  in  my  clothes,  he 
wouldn't  be  happy,  however.   But,  I  had  a  beautiful  fuchsia 
suit  that  I  bought  when  we  lived  in  Cambridge.   I  got  it  at  a 
sale,  as  always.   It  was  wool  and  it  had  a  little  navy  blue 
edging  to  it.   It  was  stunning.   It  had  a  dark  blue  blouse.   I 
came  down  in  this  suit,  and  he  said,  "Take  it  off!   It's  too 
flashy" --or  some  such  comment.   Wow!   I  was  so  amazed.   I 
didn't  know  that  he  noticed  colors. 

Now,  do  you  notice  that  nothing  in  this  room  has  got 
purples  or  fuchsias  in  it?  It  is  all  oranges?  He  does  not 
like  the  red  colors  that  have  purple  in  them.   He  doesn't  even 
like  it  in  the  garden.   I  have  had  to  push  to  get  flowers  that 
are  not  purpley,  and  so  many  flowers  have  these  purpley  colors, 
it  is  one  of  the  natural  colors. 

He  has  never  admitted  it,  but  I  think  it  goes  back  to  the 
southern  image  of  "nigger  pink"  that  my  mother  used  to  talk 
about  that  blacks  wear,  and  probably  prostitutes  as  well.   So 
in  Greenville  Charlie  saw  the  blacks  wearing  it.   Isn't  that 
interesting?   I  finally  wore  the  suit  only  when  I  was  not  going 
to  be  with  him,  and  it  finally  wore  out.   But  I  have  never 
bought  anything  more  in  that  color,  and  he  didn't  want  it  in 


625 


his  house.   I  don't  think  it  is  really  an  aesthetic  thing, 
although  he  may  think  it's  aesthetic.   That's  just  my  analysis 
of  it,  however. 

Riess:      That  sounds  likely.   That's  very  interesting. 

F.  Townes:   And  he  doesn't  like  purple,  either,  because  he  sort  of 

associates  it  with  old  ladies.   When  they're  in  mourning,  they 
wear  purple.   I  see  it  as  a  regal  color.   He's  gotten  a  little 
used  to  that.   I  have  a  little  purple  that  I  can  wear  now.   And 
I  have  a  bright  red  suit.   I  was  wearing  it  last  night.   You 
could  see  me  a  mile  away.  That  he  didn't  mind,  bright  red. 
But  the  fuchsia,  no! 


Friends.  Mentors,  and  Kindness  to  Students 


Riess:      When  you  talk  to  other  scientists'  wives,  do  you  find  that 
these  are  really  common  kind  of  things  that  you  are  dealing 
with? 

F.  Townes:   Yes,  in  my  generation.   But  I  think  the  younger  women  are  so 

much  more  independent  and  have  been  busily  out  preparing  for  or 
doing  their  careers. 

There  is  a  lot  of  competitiveness  in  science,  my  gosh!   It 
really  was  a  disillusionment,  to  me,  that  scientists  were  not 
more  objective- -any  more  objective  than  anyone  else.   Charlie 
is  really  a  passive -aggressive.   He  has  this  southern,  quiet, 
and  slow- speaking  manner,  and  all  of  that,  but  he's  all  there 
for  his  interests.   I  think  a  lot  of  successful  scientists  are 
like  that.   He  isn't  going  to  walk  roughshod  over  people, 
however . 

You  asked  me  here  something  about  who  were  his  mentors  and 
things  like  that? 

Riess:      Yes,  that  is  a  very  important  question. 

F.  Townes:   His  mentors?   He  is  not  a  person  who  has  many  close  men 

friends.   I'm  his  friend,  as  much  as  I  can  be,  and  for  that 
part  of  him  which  isn't  scientific.  There  are  the  scientists, 
but  I  don't  think  that  Charlie,  at  this  point  in  his  life,  has 
any  friend  who  would  combine  what  I  give  him  and  what  his 
scientific  friends  give  him. 

Riess:      So  how  is  a  scientist  a  friend,  then? 


626 


F.  Townee:   Veil,  they  talk  about  scientific  things  together. 

I've  often  thought  about  the  whole  question  of  who  would 
take  over  his  cloak,  his  work.  When  he  goes,  it  is  up  for 
grabs.   There  is  really  nobody  to  do  it.   There  have  been  one 
or  two  possible  people  along  the  way  among  his  students  and 
colleagues,  but  they've  all  gone  off  and  done  their  thing.   He 
felt  congenial  with  them,  but  could  they  really  have  taken  over 
his  research? 

They  talk  in  their  realm  of  science.   They  don't  talk 
about  personal  matters.   Okay,  to  just  give  a  name,  say  John, 
somebody  I  know  who  is  a  scientist,  has  had  a  new  baby.   Well, 
I  don't  find  out  until  the  baby  is  a  month  or  two  old.   "Why 
didn't  you  tell  me?"   "Well,  we  don't  talk  about  things  like 
that.   I  mean,  why  would  he  tell  me  he  has  had  a  new  baby?" 
They  wouldn't  be  into  that  whole  thing  of  family. 

Charlie  is  very,  very  supportive  of  his  students,  of 
people  who  work  with  him.   He  is  utterly  fair,  and  that  part  of 
him  is  so  superior  to  some  of  the  scientists  that  I've  seen 
whom  I  could  tell  you  stories  about- -right  here  in  Berkeley- -of 
people  who  have  cut  each  other's  throat.   Charlie  would  never 
do  that.   He  would  have  utter  integrity  in  that.   His  name,  of 
course,  begins  with  "T" .   If  it  began  with  "A"  I  think  he  still 
would  put  his  name  at  the  end  of  the  list  of  a  collaboration. 
His  students  have  been  so  impressed  that  he's  willing  to  share 
with  them,  which  you  cannot  say  of  lots  of  physicists  or 
scientists.   In  fact,  when  he  got  the  prize,  I  don't  know 
whether  he  told  you,  he  shared  the  money  with  all  of  his 
students . 

What  was  so  touching  to  me--.   Among  the  students  that  he 
sent  a  check  to  there  was  one  woman  who  had  been  very  difficult 
when  she  was  getting  her  Ph.D.  with  him,  a  Radcliffe  girl  who 
was  with  him  when  he  was  at  MIT,  Elsa  Garmire.   She  was  six- 
foot-two,  and  she  and  her  then-MIT  husband,  who  divorced  her, 
used  to  come  up  to  the  farm.   She's  now  remarried  and  has  two 
girls,  but  then  she  was  impossible.   Anyway,  she  used  to  come 
up  to  Charlie's  provost's  office  saying,  "Ray  Chiao  is  not 
treating  me  correctly,  and  the  men--."   She  was  in  the 
aggressive  feminist  mode,  and  taking  it  out  on  Charlie. 

Elsa  went  on  into  laser  art,  and  then  teaching  at 
university  level.   She's  quite  successful--!  think  she  is  the 
new  head  of  the  optical  society  now- -and  she  turned  out  to  be  a 
great  gal.   Well,  she  sent  me  back  her  check  and  said,  "Since  I 
got  married  and  had  a  family,  I  realized  how  much  you  have 
supported  Charlie  through  the  years,  and  I  want  you  to  take 


627 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 

Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 

Riess: 

F.  Townes: 

Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


this  money  and  do  something  fun  with  it."  Which  I  thought  was 
such  a  nice  touching  thing  to  do. 

How  very  nice.   The  check  that  had  been  distributed  to  her  from 
the  prize? 


Yes ,  from  the  prize . 
a  physicist. 

Very  classy. 


She  circled  back  to  me  as  a  woman,  not  as 


Not  one  of  the  men  ever  gave  any  money  back  and  said,  "We 
didn't  earn  but  part  of  this."  Charlie  got  the  prize  at  the 
absolute  bottom  of  what  it  has  been  worth,  and  he  only  had  half 
of  the  prize,  because  the  two  Russians  each  got  a  quarter  of 
it,  so  it  wasn't  any  tremendous  amount.   It  was  a  couple 
hundred  dollars  he  gave  Elsa,  'I  believe.   But  anyway,  it  was 
the  symbolism  of  it  that  meant  a  lot  to  me. 

You  talked  about  his  friends .   For  instance ,  Luis  Alvarez , 
another  Berkeley  physics  Nobelist? 

Luis  is  absolutely  an  entirely  different  person.   They  lived 
around  the  corner  but  we  weren't  close  friends. 

I  guess  it's  a  kind  of  ideal  image,  isn't  it,  of  two  great 
scientists  having  a  long  walk  and  suddenly  a  fusing  of 
brilliant  ideas  or  something? 

A  lot  of  these  guys  have  huge  egos.   It  makes  it  hard  for  them 
to  share.   Charlie  never  had  difficulty  sharing  however. 

So  they  are  happier  with  students  than  with  each  other? 

Yes  and  no.   One  pretty  good  friend  we  have  who's  a  scientist 
is  Dr.  Art  Rosenfeld,  the  physicist.   They  came  to  MIT  when 
Charlie  was  the  provost.   Charlie  admires  Art  an  awful  lot  for 
his  work  in  ecology.   And  I  admire  his  wife  Roz  [Roselyn] : 
she's  a  fine  lawyer  working  in  ecology  and  a  lovely  person. 
She  and  I  have  had  long  walks . 

But  anyway,  Charlie  isn't  your  typical  man's  man.   He 
isn't  interested  in  spectator  sports  for  example.   I  share  that 
with  him;  neither  am  I.   Our  joy  is  to  go  for  a  long  walk  to 
observe  the  birds  and  flowers. 


Riess: 


So  he  wouldn't  choose  someone  else  to  go  for  a  long  walk  with? 


628 


F.  Townes:   No,  not  usually.   He  has  scientists  he  greatly  admires- -he  was 
a  great  admirer  of  Oppenheimer,  he  knew  Sakharov.   He  knew 
these  people  and  he  admired  them.   No  long  walks,  however! 

Veil,  the  one  place  that  he's  had- -goodness ,  this  seems  so 
funny!   Up  at  the  Bohemian  Grove  he  has  a  very  good  time,  and 
there  are  very  few  scientists  up  there.   In  his  little  Isle  of 
Aves  camp  there  are  no  other  scientists- -well ,  yes  there  is 
one--.   He  got  into  the  club  there  through  Roger  Heyns  and 
other  UC  administrators.   The  head  person  of  this  camp  was  the 
dean  of  Grace  Cathedral,  the  Episcopal  cathedral.   He  was  from 
Louisiana.   He  has  just  died.   A  marvelous  guy. 

They're  a  lot  of  lawyers  in  his  camp,  and  he  gets  on  fine 
with  lawyers.   He  goes  up  there  and  he  gets  invited  around  to 
the  conservative  camps,  and  musical  camps,  and  other  camps,  and 
he  just  has  a  ball.   Somehow  that's  been  good  for  him,  and  I 
surely  encouraged  him,  although  I  felt  this  was  a  chauvinist 
group  and  pretty  extravagant.   But  he  goes  one  weekend  or  maybe 
two  weekends  a  year,  so  it  is  hardly  going  to  change  his  life, 
but  1  think  it  is  the  nearest  he  has  ever  had  to  a  fraternity. 
He  didn't  have  one  in  college.   It's  like  he's  having  something 
denied  him  early  in  his  life,  because  it  is  kind  of  like  a 
schoolboy  fraternity:  peeing  on  the  trees  and  drinking  and 
talking.   They  talk  a  lot  of  politics,  and  he  and  Roger  and  the 
others  have  a  fine  time. 

Charlie  took  his  son-in-law  who  is  the  minister  [Craig 
Anderson,  married  to  Ellen  Townes]  up  there.   Craig  just  had  a 
fabulous  time.   He  came  back  with  lawyer  stories  for  the  rest 
of  his  life.   He  and  Roger  Heyns  got  on  famously.   Roger  and 
Esther  are  very  good  Presbyterians.   Craig  and  Roger  had  a 
wonderful  time  together.   Roger's  in  charge  of  the  lakeside 
chats,  and  Charlie  has  helped  him  a  lot  with  that.   That's 
usually  what  Charlie's  contribution  is  up  there,  a  lakeside 
chat. 

So  Charlie  really  looks  forward  to  the  encampment.   That's 
the  nearest  he's  had  to  some  non-scientific  friendships,  I 
think. 


Thoughts  on  the  Washington  Scene 


Riess:      When  he  was  in  Washington,  and  when  he  was  on  boards,  did  that 
represent  a  kind  of  strain  and  struggle,  to  have  to  listen  so 
much  to  people  whose  opinions  perhaps  he  didn't  value? 


629 


F.  Townes:   I  presume  so.   I  think  the  whole  Washington  scene  is  difficult. 
Again,  I  enjoyed  it,  because  I  took  advantage  of  being  there 
and  going  to  Congress  and  getting  to  know  how  it  functioned. 
And  you  live  next  door  to  all  of  these  semi -prominent  people. 

One  of  the  people  we  lived  near  to,  his  wife  and  I  had 
been  to  the  Master's  School  together,  and  he  was  in  charge  of 
the  Bay  of  Pigs.   I  saw  him  the  day  this  was  announced.   He  was 
pushing- -they  had  a  late  baby,  and  he  was  pushing  a  stroller. 
I  said,  "Have  you  been  doing  anything  interesting  lately?" 
"No,"  he  said- -he  was,  I  think,  in  the  CIA- -and  I  opened  the 
paper  and  there  he  was!   You  never  quite  knew.   You  knew 
something  was  going  on  over  there,  but--. 

My  brother  [Newell  Brown]  was  there  at  the  same  time  and 
lived  near  us.   He  was  an  assistant  secretary  of  labor  under 
Eisenhower.   We  were  there  a  year  under  Eisenhower,  and  a  year 
under  Kennedy.   It  was  a  very  hopeful  time.   It  was  a  better 
time  to  be  there  than  now. 


But  anyway,  yes,  I  think  Charlie  would  never  have  wanted 
to  make  that  a  career,  no.   Again,  it  was  going  to  all  of  these 
parties.   It  seemed  to  me  that  government  was  made  by  people 
who  drank  too  many  martinis.   It  seemed  to  me  very,  very 
shallow.   It  was  disappointing. 

Riess:      So  how  did  he  "operate"  in  that  situation? 

F.  Townes:   He  seemed  to  do  pretty  well.   I  probably  don't  give  him  credit 
for  being  as  adaptable  as  he  is.   I  think  he  felt  he  had  a  kind 
of  mission.   When  he  has  a  feeling  of  mission,  then  he  pushes 
that  very  hard.   He  thought  there  was  a  contribution  he  could 
make  in  Washington. 

But  he  certainly  didn't  plan  to  be  provost  at  MIT.   After 
two  years  in  D.C.  we  were  going  back  to  physics  at  Columbia. 
And  then  to  go  into  this  administrative  role,  it  was  like  he 
was  moving  more  and  more  into  a  non- scientific  world,  because 
he  was  learning  skills  of  speech-making  and  writing  and  all  of 
those  kinds  of  things.   So  it  isn't  that  he  couldn't--. 

I  think  he  would  have  been  a  wonderful  president  for  MIT 
after  his  provost  experience,  but  I  think  it  would  have  been  a 
personal  sacrifice,  and  even  for  me,  though  at  the  time  it 
didn't  feel  that  way. 


630 


Decisions:  Job  Changes.  Houses,  a  Farm  in  New  Hampshire 


Riess:      How  does  he  make  decisions?  It  sounds  like  it  was  hard 

deciding  whether  to  move  on  from  Bell  Labs,  or  whether  to  give 
Bell  Labs  a  chance  to  come  through  with  some  work  that  he  could 
do.   These  and  similar  moments,  does  he  work  them  through  in 
his  own  mind? 

F.  Townes:   He  works  them  through  and  it's  always  motivated,  in  the  end,  by 
what  he  feels  his  contribution  can  be,  what  his  gift  is,  what 
he  could  do  that  maybe  somebody  else  couldn't  do. 

The  Bell  Labs  thing  was  again  a  crisis  because  we  had 
architectural  plans  to  build  a  house  out  there  in  New  Jersey, 
and  we  had  bought  land  and  we  had  eliminated  the  poison  ivy--. 
It  was  a  nice  suburban  community  in  Summit,  New  Jersey.   That 
was  very  hard.   We  moved  to  New  York,  and  Columbia  University, 
in  the  snowstorm  of  the  century  with  our  number  two  daughter- - 
Ellen  was  a  baby- -into  this  little  apartment,  and  our  furniture 
was  stuck  on  the  way,  and  we  spent  New  Year's  lying  on  the  bare 
floors  of  the  apartment. 

Here  we  were,  sort  of  starting  over  again.   So  it  was  a 
hard  time.   But  I  think  that--.   You  know,  I  thought  being  a 
professor's  wife  would  be  great,  really.   I  didn't  mind.   But 
to  give  up  that  idea  of  our  own  special  house --we've  never 
really  had  our  dream  house.  We  lived  in  apartments  until  Holly 
[Holly  Townes,  married  to  Gary  Witzel] ,  daughter  number  four, 
was  born,  which  was  a  long  time.   Then  we  bought  a  house  in  the 
West  Bronx.   We  have  owned  three  houses  in  our  lives. 

Riess:      Tell  me  about  the  farm  in  New  Hampshire  while  we  are  talking 
about  houses.   What  has  that  stood  for  in  your  lives? 

F.  Townes:   Oh,  that  is  so  significant.   That  house  was  a  way  to  get  away 
from  the  social  crush.   We  entertained  Thursday  night  in 
Cambridge,  and  then  we'd  go  to  the  farm  Friday- -it  was  near 
enough  to  Cambridge.   There  we  could  be  ourselves.   And  the 
children  would  come  up  with  us.   We  could  be  out-of-doors  and 
we  could  be  informal.   Charlie  has  this  tremendous  love  of  the 
land.   He  gets  this  from  his  father.   My  father,  too,  had  this 
love.   So  we  share  that. 

We  picked  up  this  land  for  almost  nothing. 
Riess:      You  bought  it?   It  was  not  in  your  family? 


631 


F.  Townes:   No,  no.   We  bought  it.   My  home  was  in  the  northern  part  of  the 
state,  way  up  near  Canada. 


Riess: 


When  did  you  buy  it? 


F.  Townes:  We  bought  it  in,  gosh,  1962  or  '63.   I  would  have  to  verify 
that. 


Riess:      You  were  at  MIT? 

F.  Townes:  Oh  yes.   It  was  an  MIT  thing.  We  went  looking  for  a  farm  that 
would  be  any  old  place.  We  didn't  care.   Something  primitive. 
But  we  ended  up  really  with  a  much  more  beautiful  house  than  we 
had  intended.   It  was  partly  renovated,  but  very  old.  The 
people  who  started  the  renovation  came  on  hard  times  and  had  to 
leave.   It  had  bathrooms  and  stuff,  so  we  didn't  have  to  sort 
of  camp  as  we  thought  we  might  have  to.   Then  there  was  the 
barn  and  the  land  and  it  was  just  wonderful. 

I  think  coming  out  here  was  [clouded  by  the  thought  that] 
we  would  have  to  leave  that.   We  had  a  wonderful  old  Victorian 
house  in  Cambridge,  a  famous  house  with  three  stories,  and 
lovely  for  entertaining,  right  in  the  middle  of  Cambridge,  next 
to  the  Longys  Music  School.   (Our  third  daughter  [Carla]  took 
her  cello  lessons  there.)   That  was  a  hard  house  to  leave,  too. 
But  then  I  had  this  gift--God  has  given  me  many  gifts--. 

There  was  a  friend  of  mine,  a  Smith  graduate,  a  prominent 
woman  in  the  community,  quite  a  lot  younger,  interested  in  the 
out-of-doors,  and  they  had  visited  the  farm.   I  was  going  on 
some  kind  of  tour  to  look  at  topiaries  in  Connecticut,  I 
remember,  and  Pat  was  on  the  bus.   I  said,  "Pat,  we  are  moving 
to  California.   Would  you  and  Herb  be  interested  in  renting  our 
house?"  And  she  said,  "We  would  love  to."   She  had  quite  a  lot 
of  money,  and  his  family  were  in  a  successful  lumber  business 
and  had  money. 

They  rented  our  farm  all  year  round,  but  we  could  come 
when  we  wanted  to.   That  went  on  for  many  years.   They  improved 
it,  and  they  became  friends  with  the  farmer  who  ran  it,  and  it 
is  still  part  of  their  lives.   They  are  there  for  Christmas  if 
we  are  not  there.   They  send  us  notes --he's  a  great  birder,  and 
he  writes,  "I  saw  the  bluebird,  I  heard  this--."  You  know, 
it's  been  their  home,  too. 

We  thought  we'd  have  to  give  it  up  because  we  couldn't 
afford  it.   But  we  still  have  it.   I  went  there  two  weeks  ago 
for  two-and-a-half  days.   Now  it's  the  children's  home  away 


632 


Riess: 


F.  Townes: 


from  home  as  well.   It  is  the  only  place  that  has  really  been 
home  for  us  all  because  we  have  moved  so  many  times. 

I  don't  consider  this  [San  Antonio  Avenue  house]  home, 
because  the  children  have  never  been  here,  except  Holly.   I'd 
give  it  up  tomorrow.   Charlie  wouldn't.   He  likes  his  hole-in- 
the-wall  here,  his  study.   I  don't  know  why  we  couldn't  have  a 
hole-in-the-wall  like  that  somewhere  else.   [laughter]   It  is  a 
lot  to  keep  up  an  old  house,  as  you  well  know.   I  would  miss 
it,  but  I  think--.   Charlie  knows  the  minute--if  he  goes  before 
me --that  I  would  sell  this  house,  to  have  some  flexibility,  a 
place  where  I  could  come  and  go. 

All  right.  Next? 

Okay.   All  right.   Next.   So  the  decision-making  mostly  takes 
place  within  his  inner  self? 

That's  right.   And  he  sees  an  area  where  he  can  contribute 
which  he  doesn't  think,  at  that  time,  that  other  people  can. 
And  then  if  other--.   I  don't  know  whether  you  call  that  an  ego 
thing  or  not,  but  it  is  what  keeps  him  going.   If  he's  going  to 
be  bored  he  moves  on.   All  right? 


Charlie's  Interests  Outside  of  Physics:  Travels 


Riess:      All  right.   About  interest  in  music,  novels--!  doubt  if  he  is 
interested  in  novels,  but--. 

F.  Townes:   He  likes  classical  music.   He  listens  to  it  and  poetry  in  the 
car.   He  used  to  do  a  larger  variety  of  things  than  he  does 
now.   I  think  his  life  has  tightened  up,  the  time  left  is 
shorter,  there  is  so  much  to  be  done  that  never  will  be  done, 
that's  a  lot  of  it.   I  mean,  we  used  to  go  to  opera,  we  used  to 
do  a  lot  more:  music,  go  out,  and  everything.   So  I've  had  to 
do  that  more  and  more  on  my  own,  which  I  regret,  because  we 
both  love  opera,  he  really  loves  opera.   I  bought  season  opera 
tickets  when  we  came  out  here,  and  he  never  was  available. 

He  does  not  read  novels  because  he  likes  to  read  for 
information.   His  older  sister  is  terrifically  well-read,  and 
his  younger  brother  as  well,  and  they've  said,  "It's  too  bad 
Charlie  doesn't  read  more  books."  Now,  I  am  dyslexic  and  a 
slow  reader,  and  that  has  always  been  a  problem  for  me.   So  I 
don't  read  novels  very  well  either.   I  have  read  many  novels 


633 


halfway  through,  and  the  last  page,  but  I  haven't  finished  that 
many  because  I  am  a  slow  reader. 

When  I  went  into  the  natural  sciences  [at  the  Oakland 
Museum]  that  was  a  mutual  interest  for  us.   We  read  natural 
science  information.   He  loves  for  me  to  read  aloud  to  him. 
That's  a  recreational  thing  that  we  do.   I  or  he  will  find  an 
interesting  article  that  I  will  read  out  loud,  and  then  we'll 
talk  a  little  about  it.   But  novels,  no.   Walks,  of  course, 
yes.   And  he  pursues  hobbies,  such  as  they  are,  with  great 
verve.   (Of  course,  the  whole  bird  thing  grew  out  of  his  older 
brother's  interest  in  natural  history.) 

He  [Charlie]  called  me  from  New  Zealand  the  night  before 
last.   He  had  taken  a  day  off.   He  was  so  excited  on  the  phone 
because  he  had  been  to  the  rain  forest,  and  he  had  seen  all  of 
these  things  that  we  plant  as  cultivated  plants,  like 
podocarpus  and  pittisporum,  growing  in  the  wild.   All  of  these 
plants  that  we  buy  in  pots.   And  many  exotic  birds,  and  it  was 
dripping  rain.   He  just  said  it  was  fabulous,  the  real  rain 
forest. 

Riess:      Does  he  get  an  expert  to  take  him  on  a  walk  like  that? 

F.  Townes :   He  often  does,  but  he  can  go  himself.   We  have  books  on 

everything,  you  know,  plants  and  animals:  birds  of  New  Zealand, 
for  example.   He  knows  enough  about  it  that  he  doesn't  need  to 
go  [with  someone  else].   He  went  on  his  own  in  New  Zealand.   He 
just  kept  saying,  "I  wish  you  were  here  because  you  would  have 
enjoyed  it,  too."   So  that  whole  decision  for  me  to  go  into  the 
natural  sciences  was  such  a  good  move  to  bring  us  together  in 
an  old-age  hobby.   And  having  the  new  hip,  which  meant  I  would 
continue  to  walk  well  and  all  that--.   So  I  just  pray  that 
walks  and  birding  will  be  something  that  will  continue. 

Riess:      And  why  aren't  you  in  New  Zealand  right  now?  How  do  you  decide 
which  trips  you'll  both  do? 

F.  Townes:   Oh,  well  that  is  a  good  question.   I  could  have  gone  to  India, 
too,  Charlie  would  take  me  on  any  of  these  trips  if  I  wanted  to 
go.   I  feel  very  frustrated  in  a  way.  He  would  be  flying  all 
night  for  astronomy  in  New  Zealand,  sleeping  all  day.   For  one 
day  in  the  rain  forest  I  would  have  to  go  with  him  for  almost 
two  weeks  in  New  Zealand.   I  could  do  things  on  my  own--.   I 
went  to  New  Zealand  with  him  once  before  when  he  went  to  the 
South  Pole,  and  I  explored  New  Zealand  on  my  own. 

But  to  have  some  kind  of  life  of  my  own,  I  cannot  keep 
interrupting  it,  which  I  still  do  quite  a  lot,  any  more  than  I 


634 


F.  Townes: 


Riess: 


do,  or  I  would  not  be  able  to  do  anything.   I  mean,  I  haven't 
done  anything  for  the  Council  on  Architecture  [of  the  Oakland 
Museum]  for  ages.   I  should  resign,  because  I  couldn't  come  to 
the  retreat  and  be  a  useful  member  of  the  committee.   So  to  do 
anything  of  any  value,  I  have  to  have  some  staying  power,  and  I 
had  already  been  to  India  twice,  and  had  been  with  him  on  a 
trip  similar  to  his  recent  one. 

He  still  has  difficulty  in  seeing  why  I  just  wouldn't  want 
to  go.   You  know,  you  go  and  stay  in  a  good  hotel,  and  maybe  we 
have  dinner  together.   But  then  he  would  probably  be  eating 
with  scientists  and  talking  science  all  evening.   It  just 
wouldn't  be  worth  it.   And  so  I  have  to  be  here  and  he  has  to 
be  there  often. 

It's  hard  sometimes,  because  he  feels  it  is  almost  like  my 
rejecting  him.   But  he's  going  to  go  anyway,  whether  1  go  with 
him  or  not.   I  think  maybe  it  salves  his  conscience  a  little 
bit  if  I  go?  Then  I  am  supposed  to  be  getting  something  out  of 
a  trip.   But  often  I'm  really  not,  especially  if  I've  been  to 
that  spot  before.   I  mean,  there  was  no  need  to  go  to  Paris, 
really. 

The  reason  we  went  to  Paris  was  because  all  of  our  French 
friends  had  worked  so  hard  to  get  us  this  honor.   Ve  had  to  say 
thank-you,  have  them  at  the  Elysee  Palace,  because  you  don't 
get  prizes  or  honors  unless  people  believe  in  you.   Then  my 
son-in-law  [Craig  Anderson]  came  without  Ellen  who  was  home 
with  their  new  son  Colin  Charles  [Townes -Anderson] .   He  brought 
their  older  son,  Nathan,  age  ten.   The  big  thing  for  my 
grandson  was  going  to  the  Louvre  with  Grandma.   He  loved  that. 
He  came  back  with  a  picture  of  the  Victory  of  Samothrace  for 
his  teacher.   That  was  the  thing  he  wanted  to  bring  back.   And 
Grandma  could  speak  French.   And  now  he  wants  to  learn  French 
using  Asterix  as  a  trot. 

H 

He  and  I  had  been  to  the  Egyptian  exhibit  at  the  Metropolitan, 
and  he  loved  that,  so  he  said,  "Can  we  go  to  the  Egyptian 
exhibit?"  The  Louvre  has  a  fantastic  Egyptian  exhibit.   He  has 
a  picture  of  himself  taken  with  a  Pharaoh's  foot  about  this  big 
[gestures].   So  anyway,  for  me  that  was  the  trip,  with  him  and 
Craig.   Charlie  was  lecturing  over  at  the  Ecole  Normale .   Again 
I  would  have  been  just  floating  a  lot  if  they  hadn't  come  and 
it  was  terribly  cold.   It  was  a  thrill  to  be  at  the  Elysee 
Palace,  of  course.   I'm  not  that  blase. 

I  can  imagine  that  whether  to  make  those  trips,  or  not,  is  a 
big  issue  for  scientists'  wives. 


635 


F.  Townes:   Well,  I  think  we  keep  our  balance  on  that. 

India,  I  didn't  go.  We  were  going  to  Indonesia,  to  see 
some  birds,  a  part  of  Java  where  there  is  a  special  park,  but 
it  just  turned  out  to  be  too  complicated  and  too  expensive. 
Charlie  will  never  stay  more  than  a  couple  of  days,  you  see. 

Riess:      So  the  two  of  you  don't  take  trips  where  the  only  motivation  is 
the  sights  you  will  see  rather  than  the  science? 

F.  Townes:   Not  often,  although  we  did  go  to  the  Galapagos  Islands  just  for 
natural  science.   He  would  go  for  pure  science,  and  then  for 
him  there  could  be  a  sort  of  a  nature  component.   I  could  do 
the  art  on  my  own—although  he  did  go  back  to  Delhi  recently 
and  spend  two,  three  days  and  go  back  to  the  Taj  Mahal  to  see 
it  a  second  time  and  do  those  things  which  I  would  have  enjoyed 
if  I  could  have  just  dropped  in  for  those  three  days.   But 
trips  are  very  exhausting  to  that  part  of  the  world,  I  mean  the 
jet  lag,  and  then  flying  all  over,  and  I'm  getting  older,  and 
that's  much  harder  for  me  to  do  now. 


Frances's  Math  Block 


Riess :      Speaking  of  reading  novels  and  things ,  have  you  read  science 
over  the  years?  What  have  you  done  to  make  yourself  smart  in 
physics?   [laughter] 

F.  Townes:   Less  stupid?   I'm  not  smart.   I  have  done  very  little.   I  have 
a  math  block,  a  real  math  block.   Charlie  says  when  he  talks 
math  I  have  a  little  screen  I  pull  down  in  front  of  my  eyes. 

I  took  a  course- -when  I  was  working  with  UC  Extension  for 
about  five  years  doing  a  little  counseling  of  the  women  going 
back  into  professions  I  could  take  any  course  free  and  I  took  a 
course  for  people  who  have  math  blocks,  and  I  came  out  of  it 
realizing  that  I  was  not  utterly  stupid,  which  was  a  big  plus. 
But  I  didn't  go  on  with  what  I  should  have  done,  which  was  to 
find  a  pal  with  whom  I  could  talk  about  it  on  my  level ,  and  do 
some  kind  of  math  stuff  together  regularly,  and  thus  move 
along. 

I  just  think  that  I  gave  up  a  long  time  ago,  which  a  lot 
of  women  in  my  generation  did,  so  that  what  little  science  I 
know  is  pretty  much  from  osmosis.   I  have  read  some  more 
philosophical  books  about  it,  but  I  really  know  very  little.   I 
had  one  year  of  physics  at  Smith,  and  I  took  it  because  I  was 


636 


semi -in  love  with  my  physics  teacher  because  he  could  ski.   I 
didn't  really  learn  much  except  Archimedes'  principle.   Anyway, 
that's  about  all  that  I  remember. 

And  physics  has  moved.   You  realize  in  my  lifetime  the 
exponential  growth  of  science,  so  that  the  nearest  I  got  to 
pure  science  is  natural  science.   In  fact,  1  lacked  so  much 
confidence  that  I  didn't  even  think  I  could  take  the  decent 's 
course  and  pass  it.   I  took  it  as  an  auditor  because  I  wasn't 
sure  I  could  pass  the  tests.   That's  how  much  1  lack  self- 
confidence  . 

Riess:      Charlie's  great  day  in  Franklin  Park,  when  he  was  on  the  park 

bench  in  the  midst  of  the  azaleas ,  and  then  he  had  the  idea  for 
the  maser--he  told  Art  Schawlow  about  it  back  in  the  hotel 
room,  but  when  did  he  tell  you,  and  what  did  he  tell  you,  and 
what  sense  did  it  make  to  you? 

F.  Townes:   [pauses]   It  has  become  such  a  legend  that  I  don't  know  what  is 
truth  and  what  is  fiction  about  it.   I  don't  remember  really, 
but  I  think  he  came  home  with  excitement  that  he  had  somehow 
had  some  insight  that  had  put  the  pieces  together. 

Riess:      Would  he  tell  you  the  specifics,  or  would  he  simply  say,  "I 
think  I  have  found  the  answer." 

F.  Townes:   I  don't  think  he  told  me  any  details  about  it.   I  just  think 

through  the  years  1  began  to  understand  what  masers  and  lasers 
are,  but  I  don't  know  details.   He  didn't  sit  down  and  draw  me 
a  picture.   He  would  have  if  I  had  asked  him.   But  I  have  a 
kind  of  inhibition  about  that  too.  That  I  would  be  too  dumb  to 
understand.   But  he  is  excellent  at  explaining  things  to  me  if 
I  ask.   He  could  teach  any  dodo  about  science.   He  is  good  at 
that.   So  it  isn't  his  unwillingness  to  do  that,  I  think  it  is 
my  own  feeling  of  inadequacy  about  it,  and  that  is  sad  for  me. 

I  feel  that  that's  a  whole  side  of  me  that  has  really  not 
been  developed.   Just  a  little  bit  of  it  has  been  developed 
through  the  natural  sciences,  but  the  whole  world  of  pure 
science,  no.   Although  cosmology  and  astronomy  has  got  this 
whole  philosophical  thing  which  is  very  satisfying  in  a  very 
abstract  way,  much  more  than  that  messy  stuff  in  the  laboratory 
with  all  of  those  little  wires  and  everything. 

He  takes  the  grandchildren  in  there  and  lets  them  play 
with  the  laser  and  they  see  all  of  these  wires,  but  to  me  that 
is  just  so  remote,  and  somehow  not  terribly  exciting,  that  you 
plug  all  of  these  little  things  in  and  so  forth--. 


637 


The  thing  about  science  that  I  have  learned,  and  about  his 
success  with  science- -and  this  list  of  adjectives  you  sent  is 
such  a  good  one  for  him,  let's  go  through  that:  the  persistence 
that  he  has  to  track  down  details.   This  is  not  glamorous. 
People  think  that  research  is  glamorous.   You  don't  sit  under 
an  azalea  bush  very  often  and  have  God  speak  to  you.   You  plug 
away  and  you  plug  away  and  you  plug  away  doing  ordinary  little 
things,  and  you  go  down  to  Mt.  Wilson  and  find  the  mouse  has 
chewed  up  the  wire,  and  you  have  to  find  which  wire  and  you 
have  to  clean  up  the  mouse-do  and  you  have  to  wash  the  mirrors. 

It  is  not  glamorous;  people  think  it's  glamorous,  it  is 
not.   Charlie  is  an  experimental  scientist,  and  that's  very 
different  from  being  an  Oppenheimer  and  sitting  there  with  a 
paper  and  pencil  and  having  great  ideas.   Really,  he  is  both-- 
he  has  to  have  the  concept- -but  to  put  it  through  [is  often 
boring,  hard  work].   So,  to  this  list,  I  would  add  stick-to- 
itiveness . 


Looking  at  Charlie's  Qualities:  Creativity.  Sensitivity 


Riess:      Well,  let's  spell  it  out.   I  asked  you,  "How  would  you 

associate  the  following  words  with  Charlie:   Creativity--?" 

F.  Townes:   All  right,  I'll  start  with  creativity.   Yes.   I  would  call  his 
the  creativity  akin  to  the  old  bromide  about  99  percent 
perspiration  and  1  percent  inspiration.   Cultivating  the  soil, 
the  biblical  idea,  and  then  the  seed  falls.   Charlie  would 
perhaps  call  the  seed  creativity  or  God.  That's  not  his  part. 
But  he  certainly  does  everything  to  prepare  for  the  God  part,  and 
I  think  that's  probably  what  real  creativity  is.   I  don't  think 
that  too  many  people  have  life-changing  experiences.   Paul  was 
struck  on  the  road  to  Damascus,  but  not  many  of  us  are. 

Stubborn.   Yes,  of  course.   I  mean,  if  he  didn't  have  that 
ability  to  stick  with  it. 

Distractedness .   Do  you  mean  is  he  distracted  by  things? 

Riess:      Well,  I  guess  I  was  sort  of  working  up  to  the  absent-minded 

professor  quality.   In  other  words,  is  his  mind  somewhere  else? 

F.  Townes:   Well,  it  is  a  lot  of  the  time.   There  again,  that's  a  typical 
male/female  thing.   He  has  learned,  like  all  husbands,  to  say 
"Yes,  No,  and  Do  go  on"  when  he  often  isn't  really  there.   And 
of  course  I  know  when  he  isn't,  so  I  get  annoyed  and  say,  "I'm 


638 


not  going  to  keep  talking  to  you  if  you  are  not  going  to  listen 
to  me."   [laughter] 

He  is  not  interested  in  all  of  the  little  niceties  of  the 
chaplaincy,  because  that  is  a  big  human  thing  I'm  going 
through.   He  doesn't  know  Joe  and  Jim  and  Jane  and  all  those 
people,  and  he  gets  the  names  all  confused.   So  I  try  to  spare 
him  that  some  of  the  time. 

And  I  think  he  is  getting  more  inattentive,  partially 
aging,  but  partially  his  hearing  isn't  as  good  now,  and  he  has 
been  very  stubborn  about  that.   There  seems  to  me  to  be  a 
certain  pride  that  he  has  about  wearing  a  hearing  aide ,  which  1 
don't  understand.   You  wear  glasses--.   I  realize  when  we  have 
been  separated  and  we  come  back  together  he  doesn't  hear  me, 
and  I  have  to  raise  my  voice.   It's  an  effort.   He  doesn't  get 
all  the  consonants,  so  there  is  often  a  misunderstanding. 

I  think  I'm  a  little  deaf,  too.   When  you  work  for  fifty 
years  to  be  able  to  understand  each  other,  and  then  you  have 
misunderstandings--.   I'm  having  adjustments  to  that,  but  we'll 
work  it  out. 

Patience/impatience.   He's  patient  when  it's  something  he 
can't  do  anything  about,  like  airplanes.   He  isn't  going  to  sit 
there  and  fret  that  the  airplane  is  grounded.   He  was  grounded 
for  over  a  day  because  of  a  huge  storm  in  Denver,  going  east 
this  last  trip- -a  meeting  on  masers  in  outer  space.   He  always 
has  papers  with  him,  and  he  will  work,  he  isn't  going  to  get 
upset  about  it.  He's  impatient  if  there's  something  that  can 
be  changed.   Time  is  the  most  valuable  thing  for  Charlie.   It 
is  like  the  clock  is  ticking  all  of  the  time;  we  don't  waste 
time,  it's  valuable. 

Tolerance.   I  guess,  again,  yes,  for  things  he  can't  do 
anything  about.   Intolerance  for  things  he  thinks  he  can 
change . 

Riess:      Okay,  let's  apply  that.  He's  not  tolerant  of  homosexuality. 
How  does  he  think  he  can  change  that? 

F.  Townes:   [pause]   Well,  you  know,  the  verdict  isn't  in  on  that.   Is  it 

genetic?   Is  it  cultural?  Charlie  thinks  that  it's  best  not  to 
practice  homosexuality  even  if  you  are  one.   You  ask  him  about 
it.   He  thinks  the  Bible  is  clearly  anti-homosexuality. 
Perhaps  he  can  influence  people  through  our  church . 

Riess:      So  he's  looking  at  it  scientifically? 


639 


F.  Townes:   More  morally.  And  yet  there  are  more  and  more  people  that  he 
respects  who  are  homosexual.   From  my  earliest  childhood,  one 
of  my  dearest  cousins  was  Newey  [Newell]  Jenkins,  who's  my  age, 
who  has  been  a  homosexual  from  ever.  When  I  was  at  Dobbs  Ferry 
[The  Master's  School],  that  strict  school,  I  used  to  go  up  and 
be  his  hostess  when  he  was  at  Yale  Music  School,  because  he  was 
living  with  a  man.   I  went  to  cover  up  for  him.   I  never  did 
tell  my  family.   My  father  never  let  him  in  the  house—he  might 
corrupt  my  three  brothers.   My  mother  and  my  sister  and  I 
adored  him.   He  has  had  a  life-long  companion.   He  has  a  place 
on  an  island  off  of  Florence,  Italy.   He  has  been  the  head  of 
this  wonderful,  small,  baroque  orchestra  in  New  York  City  all 
of  his  life.   We  still  correspond.   Newey' s  been  in  our  home 
many  times . 

Charlie  has  known  some  wonderful  people  who  are 

homosexual.   But  the  question  is,  "Do  you  overcome  it?"   To  my 

thinking,  he  has  a  pretty  old-fashioned  idea  about  this.   And  I 

don't  think  he's  alone,  and  that's  so  with  a  lot  of  males,  it 
seems  to  me . 


Riess: 


It's  like  fuchsia? 


F.  Townes:   Perhaps.   It's  a  very  deep  conditioning.  And  I  don't  think  he 
differentiates  between  what  is  truth,  in  my  sense,  and  what 
isn't  truth  in  this  area. 


He  took  Bonnie  [Bloom],  the  new  chaplain  who's  a  lesbian, 
to  lunch  at  the  Women's  Faculty  Club,  and  he  thinks  she's 
wonderful,  and  he  knows  I  go  to  parties  at  her  house  where  I  am 
the  only  straight  person.   So  he's  not  immovable,  but  that's  a 
part  of  him  that,  whether  he'll  live  long  enough  to  [change],  I 
doubt  it.   And  I  have  my  intolerance,  too.   I  have  intolerance 
of  his  intolerance.   You  know  what  I'm  saying? 

Riess:      Yes,  yes. 

F.  Townes:   Okay.   So  that  goes  along  with  a  certain  judgmentalness  on  his 
part,  that  you  haven't  got  the  strength  of  character  to  give  it 
up.   Well,  I  would  say  why  give  it  up?   If  it's  a  part  of  you 
that's  essential  to  you,  why  give  it  up?  Bonnie  has  a 
wonderful  woman  companion  who  shares  everything,  and  she's  a 
friend  of  mine,  too.   Kyle  gave  up  marriage,  divorced  her 
husband,  and  she  and  Bonnie  are  bringing  up  Kyle's  two  little 
boys,  which  we  have  talked  about:  "How  are  you  going  to  be 
father,  as  well  as  mother,  Bonnie?"   Two  women  and  two  little 
boys!   The  boys  do  see  their  father  every  other  weekend. 


640 


And  criticism.   Do  you  mean  how  he  takes  it,  or  how  he 
gives  it,  both  sides  of  the  coin? 

Riess:      Somehow  I  hadn't  even  thought  of  his  ever  having  to  take  it, 
except  maybe  from  you.   [laughs] 

F.  Townes:   He  is  a  bit  hypersensitive  to  my  criticism.   He  does  not  wish 
ever  to  be  put  in  a  slightly  humiliating  light.   There  is  an 
insecurity  there.  Out  in  the  world,  everyone  is  respectful  of 
him  and  he  doesn't  have  to  deal  with  that.  But  I'll  give  you  a 
couple  of  examples ,  which  are  really  funny  when  you  think  about 
it. 

Remember  that  we  grew  up  in  the  era  when  there  was  a 
division  of  labor.   We  married  with  the  idea  that  I  would  be 
the  housewife  and  do  all  of  those  things,  and  he  would  do  the 
other  things  and  he  would  earn  the  money.   So  I'm  stuck  with 
that,  even  living  in  the  liberated  woman's  world.   That's  what 
has  been  hard  for  me. 

My  four  daughters  all  have  married  men  who  carry  part  of 
that  burden;  that's  just  accepted.   They  would  not  have  married 
a  man  who  didn't  wash  dishes  or  didn't  get  meals  or  didn't  do 
laundry  or  didn't  babysit.   But  of  course  all  of  our  daughters 
have  worked,  which  I  haven't.   So  I  have  always  said  to  them, 
"Well,  your  father  didn't  want  me  to  work."   I  was  working  when 
I  married  him,  but  he  was  going  to  take  care  of  me,  so  I  gave 
up  my  j  ob . 

When  our  children  come  here,  Charlie  is  running  around 
mis -loading  the  dishwasher.   And  the  sons-in-law  all  say, 
"Frances,  we  know  he  doesn't  do  it  when  we're  not  here."   It's 
sort  of  the  family  secret,  but  they  don't  quite  dare  say 
anything. 

Recently  he  was  talking  to  a  bunch  of  Lutheran  students, 
graduate  people  at  the  Lutheran  chapel- -they  are  having  a 
religious  talk  series--.   You  know,  he's  part  of  this 
organization  that's  going  to  plan  the  future  of  the  world. 
It's  based  in  France.   He  has  to  give  a  paper  on  this,  and 
there  is  going  to  be  a  meeting  at  Harvard  with  the  head  of  the 
divinity  school.  He  was  sort  of  trying  this  paper  out  on  them. 

Riess:      What  is  the  organization? 

F.  Townes:   Well,  it's  this  Marois  person:  What  kinds  of  things  should  we 
be  thinking  about  for  the  future  of  the  world?  All  of  the 
scientists  and  humanists  are  getting  together,  and  the 


641 


Riess: 
F.  Townes 


philosophers.   Marois  came  to  this  reception  in  Paris  for  the 
Legion  of  Honor,  and--.   I  don't  know,  there  are  a  couple  of 
these  group  discussions  going  on  now,  the  French  are  great  at 
these  great  big  ideas.   Charlie's  involved  with  that,  helping 
to  find  people  in  different  fields  to  be  part  of  this  group. 

So  on  the  way  back  from  Paris  he's  writing  his  paper, 
starting  way  back  with  the  Big  Bang,  and  he  brings  evolution 
all  the  way  up  to  the  present.   It's  quite  a  wonderful  paper. 
Then  his  religious  side  comes  out- -this  is  a  semi-religious 
group- -that  there  are  moral  decisions  we  have  to  make,  value 
decisions  for  the  future.  He  did  a  nice  job  on  the  paper. 
It's  very  understandable  to  non-scientists  like  me. 

[laughing]   I'm  digressing.   Something  happened  that  night 
that  upset  him.   When  we  came  in,  the  girl  students  were  in  the 
kitchen  getting  the  dinner.   I  said,  "Well,  isn't  this 
interesting,  I  notice  that  the  women  are  doing  all  the  kitchen 
work."   [laughs]   They  laughed  and  I  said,  "Oh,  it's  like  my 
house."  And  that  put  him  in  a  bad  light  because  that  said  that 
he  didn't  do  much  to  help  me  in  the  house.   However,  when  the 
children  were  small  and  we  didn't  have  a  dishwasher,  he  helped 
me  wash  dishes  every  night. 

He  is  sensitive  to  that. 

Yes.   He  really  knows  in  his  deep  heart  that  he  isn't  that 
helpful  on  the  domestic  side.   So  I  got  dressed  down  for  that. 
But  I  shouldn't  have  said  anything  like  that  in  public.   That's 


my  dark  side!   Why  do  we  hurt  those  we  love  the  most! 
would  not  do  that  to  me . 


Charlie 


Later  in  the  evening  I'm  sitting  there--!  have  some  ideas 
about  the  future  of  the  world,  too- -and  I  was  just  sitting 
there.   And  finally  Reverend  Gus  Schultz,  who  was  one  of  my 
backers  when  I  started  the  chaplaincy,  and  a  very  progressive 
person,  very  liberal,  goes  to  El  Salvador  and  all,  he  finally 
looked  up  and  looked  at  me  and  said,  "You  know  people,  Frances 
is  a  person  in  her  own  right.   She  started  this  wonderful 
chaplaincy,  and  it's  a  great  thing  here  in  Berkeley,  and  I 
think  you  ought  to  know  that."  That  felt  good  to  me. 

I  have  been  working  on  this  with  Foster  because  I  know  how 
to  push  his  [Charlie's]  buttons.   I'm  very  tempted,  and 
sometimes  I  do  it- -the  devil  in  me .   I  can  do  it  just  the  way  I 
did  it  that  night.   All  I  needed  to  say  was,  "Oh,  that's  the 
way  it  is  in  my  house,"  and  he  is  upset.   I  am  putting  him  in 
this  less-than-perfect  role.   Certainly  at  home  he's  really 
sure  that  I  love  him  not  for  all  the  things  he  did,  or  the 


642 


medals  he  won.   That's  what  being  a  loving  wife  is.   Sometimes 
I  wish  he  wouldn't  be  quite  so  sensitive,  however.   It's  hard. 

He's  also  shy.   Does  he  look  at  you  when  you're  talking  to 
him? 

Riess:      When  interviews  start  often  he's  looking  out  the  window. 
Usually  at  some  point- - 

F.  Townes:   Finally  he'll  come  back. 
Riess:      --he  comes  around. 

F.  Townes:   But  he  has  a  really  hard  time,  it  seems  to  me,  looking  right  at 
you.   I'm  used  to  looking  right  at  the  person,  and  I  hear  them 
better  if  I  look  straight  at  them. 

I  think  through  the  years  people  have  approached  him, 
because  he's  well-known,  to  ask  him  to  do  things.   He  can  smell 
it  a  mile  away.   He  sort  of  absents  himself.   He's  shy.   He's 
really  shy,  except  in  areas  where  he  can  blossom  and  talk  with 
the  fellow  scientists  and  other  congenial  folk.   However  now, 
[laughs]  I  think  he's  getting  to  be  a  little  overexpansive 
sometimes.   I'm  sitting  at  the  other  end  of  the  dinner  table  at 
our  house,  and  he's  being  expansive,  and  people  like  it,  but  I 
find  myself  reduced  again  to  the  hostess,  just  watching  this 
scene  happen  and  not  feeling  that  I  have  much  to  do  with  it. 


Charlie's  Thought  Processes 


Riess:      How  is  he  different  when  he's  hard  at  work  on  something  from 
when  he's  not? 

F.  Townes:   He's  sitting  in  there  and  it  looks  like  he's  doing  nothing. 
Riess:      In  where? 

F.  Townes:   In  his  study.   I  only  see  him  there,  not  at  work.   He  would  be 
more  quiet  here  than  at  work  because  the  phone  isn't  ringing 
much.   And  he  does  not  wish  to  be  interrupted.   If  I  come  in, 
or  run  in  and  have  an  idea  or  something- -"Can't  you  see  I'm 
working?  Please  don't  disturb  me.   You  may  think  I'm  asleep, 
but  I'm  not."   So  I've  learned  through  the  years  to  be 
respectful  of  that. 


643 


Riess:  I  also  was  thinking  that  sometimes  he's  really  onto  something, 
and  sometimes  he's  just  waiting  for  something  to  begin  to  cook 
in  his  head.  Whether  that  puts  him  in  kind  of  an  offish  mood, 
so  he's  more  difficult  to  live  with  maybe. 

F.  Townes:   There  are  times  that  I  call  "walking  on  eggs  time,"  when  I  know 
that  nothing  I'm  going  to  say  is  going  to  be  appropriate.   I 
used  to  feel  that  I  ticked  him  off.   Now  I  know  it's  not  so 
much  that  he's  in  the  creative  mood,  but  that  he's  had  more 
than  he  could  possibly  handle  that  day  almost- -of  people,  angry 
or  upset  people,  or  the  University  has  fouled  up  his  account 
again,  or  his  secretary- -you  know  what  life  is  like.   I've 
finally  learned  to  deal  with  that.   It  took  me  a  long  time  to 
know  why  that  was  going  on  and  not  take  it  as  a  personal 
affront.   I  get  that  way  myself  sometimes,  and  anything  Charlie 
says  ticks  me  off. 

In  terms  of  his  thinking,  I  see  all  these  numbers  on  the 
piece  of  paper,  or  doodlings.   I  think  he's  trying  to  figure 
out  what  the  next  jump  will  be.   You  just  wonder  what's  going 
on  in  his  head.   Must  be  kind  of  exciting. 

Riess:      Yes!   Don't  you  think  so? 

F.  Townes:   I  think  so.   Must  be  marvelous.   I  think  those  are  wonderful 

moments  for  him.   I'm  not  sure  that  that  always  happens  to  him, 
but  he  certainly--.   I  would  meditate,  to  hope  that  I  had  some 
guidance.   But  I  think  his  is  a  very  different  way  of  trying  to 
make  the  synapses  all  meet.   I  would  be  opening  myself  up  to 
let  God  in.   We  have  each  a  different  way  of  letting  God  into 
our  lives.   I  think  it  happens  to  both  of  us. 

Riess:      You  said  earlier  that  99  percent  is  preparing  the  soil. 

F.  Townes:   That's  what  he's  been  doing,  and  hoping  that  those  things  would 
suddenly  meld  and  that  insight  would  be  given.   And  I'm  sure  it 
is.   The  students  tell  me  it  happens  all  the  time.   Sometimes 
it's  the  most  simple  thing.   I  hear  him  on  the  phone  perhaps 
saying,  "Have  you  screwed  that  thing  three  times  instead  of 
twice?"  "Well,  we  didn't  think  about  that."  And  they  do  it, 
and  it  works.   He  seems  to  have  an  ability  to  see  these  sorts 
of  mundane  things  that  others  don't  think  about.   It  isn't  all 
highfalutin  mathematics. 


644 


Dauehter.  Like  Father 


F.  Townes:   He's  an  introvert,  I'm  an  extrovert. 

Riess:      You  are  answering  my  question  about  motivation  and  psychology? 

F.  Townes:   Yes.   I  don't  think  psychology  is  a  word  he  understands  in  the 
way  1  do.   He's  not  self -analytical,  at  least  outwardly. 

Riess:      Have  you  ever  had  conversations  in  which  you  might  say  to  him, 
"Well,  you  do  this  because  your  father  did  it"?   Sort  of  pop- 
psychology.   Would  that  be  something  that  would  annoy  him? 

F.  Townes:   We  don't  talk  about  his  psychology. 

Our  oldest  daughter  [Linda  Rosenwein] ,  whom  I've  recently 
visited,  is  so  much  like  Charlie.   She  has  a  very  inquisitive 
mind,  like  his,  which  for  me  wasn't  easy  when  she  was  growing 
up- -it  was  like  living  with  two  Charlie  Towneses! 

Now  Linda  has  two  higher  degrees  and  a  wonderful  husband, 
a  Jewish  social  psychology  professor.   They  are  just  meant  for 
each  other:  they're  both  messy,  they're  both  intellectual, 
they're  both  very  liberal.   No  children.   He  has  MS,  though  he 
is  not  in  a  wheelchair  and  he  drives,  and  plays  the  piano.   He 
does  have  allergies  and  ailments,  but  I  think  perhaps  I'm 
overly  sensitive  on  that  subject  because  I  had  a  mother  who  had 
many  ailments. 

I  think  my  mother  was  a  bit  of  a  hypochondriac.   She  cried 
and  complained  a  lot,  and  she  had  doctors  for  her  ailments  all 
her  life.   She  actually  lived  to  be  ninety- two,  and  died  of  old 
age.   I  don't  cry,  except  surreptitiously- -Charlie  doesn't 
appreciate  tears.   I  have  turned  out  like  my  father.   He  was 
very  uncomplaining  and  never  cried. 

Charlie  understands  Linda,  he  and  Linda  understand  each 
other,  and  she  talks  to  him  a  lot.   He  has  said  to  me,  "I  know 
the  two  of  you  are  different,  but  you  have  got  to  build  a 
bridge  to  your  daughter."  And  that  was  very  good  advice  from 
him. 


645 


I  recently  took  a  trip  to  spend  time  with  the  Rosenweins 
to  see  if  I  could  build  a  bridge  to  Linda,  and  work  out  these 
problems  1  seemed  to  be  having  with  her.   And  I  am  so  glad  I 
went  because  we  had  a  fine  opportunity  to  have  a  serious  and 
healing  talk. 

I  told  Linda  how  hard  it  had  been  for  me  to  hear  from  her 
that  1  had  done  some  less  than  wise  things  during  her  growing 
up  years --remember,  she  is  a  developmental  psychologist- -and  1 
told  her  1  had  been  on  a  real  guilt  trip  about  this  for  a  long 
time.   1  wanted  to  rid  myself  of  this  guilt  now  that  she  was 
nearly  forty-nine,  and  I  was  nearly  seventy-six! 

Linda  responded  that  she  didn't  want  to  put  a  guilt  trip 
on  me.   1  responded  that  that  was  what  I  felt  she'd  been  doing. 
I  said,  "You're  the  brightest  of  my  four  daughters.   You  had 
every  advantage.   You  were  the  first  child,  you  had  everything. 
1  did  my  best  as  a  mother."  Veil,  we  hugged  and  there  was  real 
forgiveness  and  love  between  us . 

Charlie  is  so  loving  with  all  our  girls.   Each  one  knows 
that  their  father  really  cares  about  them,  with  all  their 
idiosyncracies.   In  some  ways  1  think  the  three  younger  girls 
[Ellen  Anderson,  Carla  Lumsden,  Holly  Townes-Witzel]  have 
always  been  more  my  daughters,  and  Linda  is  more  Charlie's 
daughter.   Linda  was  a  first  child,  as  was  1--1  was  one  of 
five- -and  like  Linda  I  always  had  a  very  close  relationship 
with  my  father. 

It's  interesting.   I  think  Charlie's  daughters  have  taught 
him  an  awful  lot  about  women.   And,  of  course,  they  have  taught 
me  an  awful  lot  about  being  a  professional,  and  an  emancipated 
woman.   Things  do  finally  work  out  with  your  children- -at  least 
they  have  for  me. 


646 


A  Good  Marriagetftf 


F.  Townes:   So  that's  part  of  the  question  about  what  kind  of  husband  and 
father  he  is.   He  is  a  loving  father,  a  committed  father.   I 
worried  a  lot  about  whether  my  children  would  find  husbands
