because  they  would  not  find  a  man  like  him.   They've  chosen  men 
very  different  from  their  father.   But  they  love  him  a  lot,  and 
he  gives  a  lot  to  them.   He's  not  sentimental,  but  he's 
affectionate  with  them. 

We're  lovers;  that's  been  one  of  our  natural  gifts  to  each 
other.   So  that's  been  a  very  important  part  of  our  lives,  as 
I'm  sure  it  is  in  any  good  marriage. 

Riess:      If  he  were  sitting  here  how  would  he  feel  that  you  had  just 
said  that  to  me?  You  must  be  so  different  from  him. 

F.  Townes:   He's  very  private.   I  should  have  said  he's  very  affectionate. 
He  puts  his  arm  around  me  a  lot.   And  that's  unusual,  because 
most  men  like  him  wouldn't  be  so  demonstrative.   And  he  always 
kisses  me  when  he  leaves  and  returns.   I  am  very  fortunate  to 
have  that  kind  of  relationship. 

There's  little  sweet  talk,  little  sentimentality,  but 
thoughtful,  unusual  gifts.   He  brought  me  that  lovely  rug  from 
India,  a  piece  of  jewelry,  he  likes  to  give  me  earrings.   But 
the  division  of  labor  is  always  there.   It  could  have  been 
different,  and  I've  wished  often  it  was,  but  I've  accepted  that 
with  a  certain  amount  of  grace  and  realized  that  that  was  part 
of  the  way  it  was  to  be,  especially  for  my  generation. 

When  I  look  at  marriage --so  many  of  my  friends'  husbands 
have  left  them  for  other  women,  even  in  middle  life,  and 
they've  had  to  start  over.   It's  been- -what  could  I  say?   I 
think  the  best  way  to  describe  it  is  that  I  have  never  had  to 
meet  Charlie  at  the  door  with  a  double  martini  because  he 
didn't  like  his  profession.   Yes,  some  days  are  not  so  good. 
But  he  doesn't  need  to  be  rescued  from  some  mundane  job.   He 
loves  his  work.   As  he's  said  to  you  again  and  again,  it  is  his 
hobby.   So  he  is  a  really  blessed  man. 

I  would  much  rather  be  married  to  somebody  like  that  who 
has  had  less  time  for  me,  in  a  sense,  or  perhaps  less  ability 
to  understand  my  kind  of  person,  although  I'm  not  sure  that 
these  husbands  that  have  left  their  wives  have  really 
understood  them  any  better  than  Charlie  has  understood  me. 
Somehow  they  haven't  been  able  to  move  in  relationship.   I've 


647 


done  most  of  the  pushing  toward  trying  to  get  us  to  understand 
each  other  better  in  our  home. 


Frances:  Measuring  Up  and  a  New  Friend 


F.  Townes: 

Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 

Riess: 

F.  Townes: 

Riess: 


I  watched  A  Doll's  House  by  Ibsen  last  night, 
that? 

I  watched  some  of  it. 


Did  you  watch 


It's  been  so  long  since  I  read  it.   And  I  thought,  what  a 
statement  on  women's  lib.   She  really  leaves  him  because  he  is 
treating  her  like  a  doll.   Charlie  always  says,  "You  feel 
inferior  intellectually.   You  don't  need  to."  That  goes  back 
to  my  own  psychology  of  feeling  I  was  a  dumb  bunny,  and  I'm 
sort  of  putting  myself  down.   I  bring  that  on  myself. 

And  fairly  recently  it  was  Linda,  my  oldest  daughter,  who 
discovered  that  I  was  dyslexic.   All  these  years  I  thought  the 
reason  I  read  slowly  was  because  I  was  dumb,  and  it  turns  out 
dyslexia  was  the  reason.   Charlie  has  said,  "You  have  plenty  of 
brains,  and  most  of  us  do,  and  there's  no  reason  for  you  to 
feel  stupid."  But  I  said,  "Isn't  it  ironic  that  most  of  my 
women  friends  have  always  been  a  great  deal  smarter  than  me." 
I've  always  been  attracted  to  women,  like  you,  who  are  very 
intelligent. 

I  like  the  stimulation  of  very  bright  people.  And  I  don't 
think  I  would  have  ever  married  a  dumb  person.   I  like  to  be 
challenged  by  intellectual  things.   And  I  suppose  that  was  part 
of  my  youth,  my  family  having  intellectual  interests  so  I  like 
intellectual  things.   Yes,  I  made  a  good  marriage. 

I  sit  here  listening  to  what  you  say,  and  not  denying  it,  but  I 
think  I  would  like  to  categorically  deny  anything  that  you  say 
about  yourself  that,  "I  am  less-- 

--of  a  person." 

Yes.   I  don't  believe  it,  Frances. 

But  I  think  that  when  you  live  in  an  academic  world,  where 
truth  is  a  certain  kind  of  truth--. 

There's  a  lot  of  measuring. 


648 


F.  Townes:   There's  a  lot  of  measuring.  When  I  wrote  that  slip  for  you 
about  my  education,  it's  so  sparse.   Charlie  has  all  these 
academic  degrees  and  honorary  degrees,  too.  And  my  children 
have  all  these  degrees.   Mine  is  so  sparse.   So  that  when 
people  ask,  "What  do  you  do?"  or  "What  is  your  education?"  they 
don't  mean,  "What  is  your  personal  growth  through  the  years? 
What  have  you  given  to  the  community  that  maybe  isn't  so 
intellectual  but  is  valuable?"  That  isn't  what  they're  asking 
you.   So  by  marrying  Charlie,  1  exposed  myself  to  that  kind  of 
thing . 

It's  a  weird  thing.   It's  like  being  a  moth  attracted  to  a 
flame.   You're  attracted  by  all  of  that.   The  academic  world, 
in  spite  of  its  limitations  and  rivalries,  is  a  very  exciting 
place.   And  Berkeley  has  all  of  that.   It's  exciting,  because 
where  could  I  have  done  a  ministry  for  the  homeless?  And  also, 
where  could  I  have  been  in  the  academic  world?  Where  could  I 
go  to  the  French  section  [of  the  UC  Section  Club]  and  talk 
French?  Where  could  I  go  to  see  good  opera  and  fine  art 
galleries? 

I  have  a  new  friend;  I  finally  have  made  a  friend  in 
Berkeley  who  is  much  like  me,  and  near  my  own  age,  and  it's 
been  the  greatest  gift.   And  where  did  I  find  this  person  but 
through  the  chaplaincy!   She's  the  wife  of  a  retired  electrical 
engineering  professor  at  Cal.   She's  a  poet,  she's  an  artist, 
and  she's  a  beautiful  woman  inside.   She  has  this  dedication  to 
liberal  ideas,  more  than  I  do.   She  found  me  and  I  found  her. 

She's  my  vice-president  now,  of  the  homeless  chaplaincy, 
Phyllis  Smith,  Mrs.  Otto  Smith.   She  is  my  dear  friend.  We're 
both  so  busy  we  don't  see  each  other  that  much,  but  when  we  do 
see  each  other  it's  wonderful.   She's  written  poems  for  me. 
She  wrote  me  a  poem  for  our  fiftieth  wedding  anniversary.   They 
had  their  fiftieth.   I  went,  and  she  introduced  me  as  her  new 
friend.   She  and  her  husband  met  at  Stanford.   She  only  has  a 
BA,  she  didn't  go  on  to  a  higher  degree.   She's  brought  up  four 
children. 

Riess:      A  lot  of  parallels. 

F.  Townes:   Everything,  except  some  of  her  children  have  been  divorced  and 
remarried,  which  ours  haven't. 

We  have  been  able  to  share.   She  adores  her  husband,  Otto, 
who's  older.   He's  very  bright,  an  inventor.   They  just  went 
off  to  Poland,  to  Krakow,  where  she  was  part  of  a  team.   She 
learned  enough  Polish  to  help  him- -he's  in  ecology. 


649 

Apparently,  the  pollution  in  Krakow  is  so  incredible  they  both, 
with  white  hair,  came  back  with  yellow  hair  they  can't  get  rid 
of,  and  they  could  have  gotten  black  lung  disease,  trying  to 
help  the  Poles.   She  was  paid  to  go.   She's  not  as  vigorous  as 
I  am,  even  though  she's  a  few  years  younger- -but  few  people 
are.   And  they  have  a  place  in  the  country  as  we  do. 

Phyllis  has  been  God's  gift  to  me,  but  1  have  known  very 
few  women  like  her  here.   1  have  younger  friends  that  I  admire 
a  lot  who  like  me  because  I  have  somehow  for  my  age  a  somewhat 
younger  point  of  view  about  life. 

Bonnie,  the  new  chaplain,  is  now  a  good  friend.   She's  the 
age  of  my  third  daughter,  they're  two  weeks  apart.   We're  able 
to  talk  about  lesbianism,  all  that  world  that  she's  in.   I'm 
going  at  11:30  to  meet  with  her  and  the  other  chaplain.   We've 
been  trying  to  find  where  I  best  fit  in  the  chaplaincy—I've 
been  sort  of  doing  little  bits  of  everything  in  the  chaplaincy 
to  date,  so  we're  exploring  where  I  could  have  a  really 
responsible  part  of  it  that  would  be  more  professional. 


"Suddenly  Feminized"- -1967 


Riess:      Please  tell  me,  back  in  1967  when  you  came  out  to  Berkeley,  how 
you  decided  what  you  wanted  to  do  here.   When  you  came  out, 
your  daughters  didn't  come  with  you? 

F.  Townes:   Three  of  them  were  in  college.   Linda  was  getting  her  doctoral 
in  psychology  at  Michigan,  Ellen  was  getting  her  master's  in 
anatomy  at  Berkeley  and  living  on  her  own,  and  Carla  was  off  at 
Scripps. 

Holly  was  fifteen,  and  went  into  the  public  schools  in 
Berkeley,  where  she  had  all  kinds  of  terrible  experiences.   She 
had  come  out  here  hoping  to  make  friends  with  blacks,  and  she 
was  beaten  up  the  day  Martin  Luther  King,  Jr.  was  killed.   She 
was  very  upset,  because  she'd  met  him  [King]  in  Sweden,  and  at 
age  eleven  went  around  saying,  "My  king  is  Martin  Luther  King, 
not  the  king  of  Sweden!"   She  had  all  these  idealisms  that  she 
was  working  through. 

And  I  was  working  through  being  in  Berkeley.   We  had  a 
difficult  year  together.   I  was  trying  to  find  my  way,  and 
Holly  was,  and  Charlie's  MIT  secretary,  Paula,  who  came  out 
with  us,  was  too.   I  persuaded  her  that  she  could  help  Charlie 
get  started  and  she  might  find  a  husband  here.   And  she  did 


650 


finally,  at  the  age  of  forty-one  or  so,  find  another  Greek  and 
marry  him  and  have  two  children  and  that  was  a  success.   So 
Paula  and  Holly  and  I  were  floundering  around  in  this  crazy 
place. 

Charlie  was  scrambling  to  see  if  he  could  get  a  toe  back 
in  science,  and  he  could  not  be  disturbed  if  he  was  going  to 
make  it.   So  we  were  all  on  thin  ice  for  that  year. 

Then  1  applied  to  the  Library  School  and  was  turned  down, 
and  I  became  suddenly  feminized.   I  was  put  on  the  committee  to 
plan  the  Women's  Center  for  Continuing  Education.   That  was 
Roger's  idea,  that  I  would  be  the  one  layperson,  because  I  had 
felt  rejected  by  the  system.   That  committee  started  the 
center. 

Riess:      Now  wait.   "Suddenly  feminized." 

F.  Townes:   Well,  I  mean  I  suddenly  saw  that  women  were  not  given  their 
due.   They  had  no  advocacy  for  women  on  this  campus. 

Riess:      And  Roger  was  Roger  Heyns? 

F.  Townes:  Roger  Heyns  was  then  the  chancellor.  He  set  up  a  committee  to 
find  an  enabling  way  for  women  on  campus.   It  started  out  to  be 
a  center  for  continuing  education  for  women.   What  I  was  trying 
to  do,  to  go  back  to  school,  there  was  no  way  that  women  were 
being  helped  with  that.   And  so  that's  the  way  the  Women's 
Center  started. 

Riess:      So  it  was  in  social  conversations  with  Roger? 

F.  Townes:   Yes,  I  guess.   I  was  outraged,  and  I  made  such  a  big  noise  that 
I  was  the  one  non-academic  person  put  on  that  committee.   And 
then  it  got  started  and  it  grew  into  the  Center  for  Women,  and 
for  undergraduates  as  well,  and  not  just  to  help  older  women  go 
back  to  school.   It  grew  and  went  on  and  finally  became  a  place 
of  advocacy  for  women  of  all  ages  and  ethnic  backgrounds .   They 
still  don't  have  an  appropriate  number  of  women  faculty,  that 
hasn't  changed.   It's  shocking. 

I  was  so  upset  by  the  fact  that  the  West  wasn't  way  ahead 
of  the  East.   You  see,  I  had  already  helped  set  up  classes  on 
the  East  Coast  for  faculty  wives  to  go  back  to  school.   There 
was  nothing  here  like  that.   I  was  amazed  how  little  progress 
there 'd  been  in  helping  women  at  this  so-called  progressive 
institution.   I  still  think  it's  a  very  chauvinist  place.   In 
many  ways  I  sympathize  with  Therese  [Heyman]  because  she  has 
fought  to  be  herself--!  saw  her  again  last  night.   She  married 


651 


this  dynamic  man,  and  to  be  in  his  shadow,  no  way  was  she  going 
to  do  that.   I  understand  that  part  of  Therese  very  well. 

Riess:      It  seems  to  me  that  at  about  that  time  Babette  Chamberlain- -you 
must  have  known  her- -was  starting  consciousness-raising  groups. 

F.  Townes:   Owen's  first  wife? 
Riess:      I  think  so. 

F.  Townes:   And  she  divorced  him.   And  then  he  married  June  Steingart,  a 
fine  artist,  a  wonderful  woman- -she  died  only  two  or  three 
months  ago --and  a  friend  of  mine.   Yes,  Babette  Chamberlain  was 
Owen's  first  wife  and  she  was  very  progressive. 

Riess:      Did  you  become  part  of  that? 

F.  Townes:   No,  I  didn't.   No,  I  never  did.   I  came  in  through  this  other 
door.   I  did  know  some  of  the  women  on  campus  who  were 
emancipated- -there's  a  physics  wife  that  is  a  professor  of 
psychology,  and  she  and  I  often  talk  about  problems  for  women 
on  campus.   Wherever  we  went,  it  was  in  the  air. 

Oh,  and  I  should  say  that  I  helped  start  a  section  of  the 
Faculty  Wives  Club  called  "Women's  Concerns."  And  we  met  at 
the  Women's  Center  to  talk  about  making  our  lives  more 
professional . 

Remember,  I  was  at  MIT  when  Betty  Friedan  wrote  her  book, 
and  Gloria  Steinem  was  around.   That  was  all  starting  then.   I 
met  Betty  Friedan.   Betty  and  Gloria  went  to  Smith- -as  I  did. 
Some  us  went  right  on  being  Smithies.   But  I  was  affected  by 
Betty  Friedan's  book,  very  much.   I  came  out  here  with  these 
aspirations,  and  felt  really  put  out  by  the  fact  that  this 
wasn't  going  on  here,  that  there  was  no  one  here  to  say,  "You 
can  be  a  person  in  your  own  right.   You're  not  just  the  wife  of 
a  Nobel  prize  winner." 

I  didn't  want  to  go  to  section  clubs  and  just  do  that.   I 
was  in  the  music  section  for  a  little  while,  it  met  here 
occasionally.   And  the  Italian  section.   I  left  both  the 
Italian  and  French  sections,  but  then  went  back  to  the  French 
section  later  because  I  didn't  want  to  lose  my  French.   But  I 
didn't  want  to  be  part  of  that  milieu.   I  had  been  head  of  the 
matrons  at  MIT,  the  same  kind  of  group  that  did  all  those 
interest  groups.   I  didn't  want  to  continue  in  that  mode.   But 
I  did  go  to  the  "Women's  Concerns"  section,  until  it  stopped 
because  all  of  us  had  found  satisfactory  niches. 


652 


Fran  and  Charlie's  Shared  Interest  in  Natural  Sciences 


F.  Townes:   I  wanted  to  do  something  new!   But  what  could  it  be?   So  I 

fiddled  around,  and  certainly  Library  School  would  have  been 
the  worst  place  for  me,  I  see  now,  because  I'm  a  slow  reader. 
I  thought,  "What  could  I  do  that  would  be  parallel  to  Charlie?" 
It  would  be  something  we  could  do  together.   I  loved  nature, 
and  we  both  loved  the  out-of-doors.  His  birdwatching  had  sort 
of  lain  fallow,  and  here  was  a  whole  new  bunch  of  birds. 

So  I  joined  the  Golden  Gate  Audubon  Society.   And  they 
sent  out  an  invitation  to  people  on  their  list  of  members  to 
find  docents  for  the  first  class. 

Riess:      Docents  for  the  first  class  of--? 

F.  Townes:   For  the  Natural  Science  Gallery  [at  the  Oakland  Museum].   So  I 
thought,  "My  goodness,  I  can  go  to  school  and  I  can  learn 
something.   I  can  come  home  and  tell  Charlie  about  the  plants 
and  the  birds."   I  went  in  with  that  in  mind,  and  then  became  a 
decent  much  to  my  surprise. 

Then  I  went  on  to  take  some  leadership  jobs,  like 
president  of  the  Decent  Council,  and  so  other  parts  of  me  were 
used,  and  it  was  a  home  away  from  home  for  me.   I  really  felt 
that  last  night,  because  it  was  so  good  to  see  some  of  my  old 
pals  down  there.   Dolly  Dreiman  said  to  me,  "You  were  in  the 
first  class,  Fran."   I  said,  "Yes."   "I  remember  you  took  me  on 
my  first  tour,  and  I  was  in  the  second  class,  and  you  showed  me 
how  it  should  be  done."  How  many  years  ago?  Twenty-two  or  - 
three . 

And  Charlie  said,  "Yes,  you  are  a  teacher.  You  didn't 
know  that."   So  I  found  that  I  could  relate  as  a  teacher.   It 
was  a  very  exciting  time  for  me  and  I  feel  that  it  was  God's 
gift  to  me,  that  whole  place. 

Have  I  told  you  this  story,  that  nobody 'd  heard  of  Charlie 
in  the  natural  science  department  because  we  never  talked  about 
our  husbands,  we  were  there  to  do  this  work  of  docenting.   But 
after  two  years  Don  came  to  me  and  said,  "Are  you  related  to 
the  famous  Henry  Townes?"  Did  I  ever  tell  you  this  story? 

Riess:      No.   Which  Don? 

F.  Townes:   McNeil.   He  was  an  entomologist.   No,  this  was  Don  Linsdale. 
Both  of  them  were  entomologists.   I  said,  "Oh  yes,  I'm  his 


653 


sister-in-law."  So  I  became  the  sister-in-law  of  the  famous 
Henry  Townes  who  was  the  world's  authority  on  ichneumonidae . 

Don  asked  me  to  write  to  him  for  a  specific  insect  for  the 
rotten  log  case.   Do  you  remember  the  little  case  in  the  High 
Sierra  with  the  grylloblattid  in  it?  It's  not  there  anymore. 
Gryllo  means  "cricket*  and  blattid  means  "roach."   It's  a  large 
insect  that  they  have  in  the  High  Sierra,  and  it  lives  under 
the  ice.   It  has  a  very  limited  habitat  and  there  are  special 
insects  above  and  below  the  ice. 

Anyway,  Don  wanted  a  parasitic  wasp  for  the  log,  the 
decaying  log.   And  I  wrote,  and  Henry  sent  one.   Whether  they 
ever  used  it,  I  don't  know.   But  for  a  long  time  [at  the 
museum]  I  was  supposed  to  know  something  about  insects,  which  I 
didn't.   That  was  heavy  enough  I   Then  one  day  they  found  I  was 
married  to  Charlie,  the  fact  was  out,  but  by  then  I  had  become 
"Fran,"  and  I  was  appreciated  for  my  own  self. 

The  rest  of  the  grylloblattid  story  was  that  when  I  first 
knew  Charlie,  he  took  me  to  meet  Henry.   Henry  was  very--.   He 
was  interested  only  in  the  taxonomy  of  insects.   Marjorie,  his 
wife,  was  a  botanist,  but  had  given  it  up  and  collaborated  with 
him,  and  they  collected  together  all  over  the  world.   A  very 
congenial  partnership.   Marjorie  is  a  very  wonderful,  wonderful 
woman.   Henry  was  very  much  a  Townes.   He  didn't  have  Charlie's 
broad  view  of  the  world,  however.   Thank  goodness  Charlie 
didn't  go  into  entomology,  at  least  into  taxonomy.   That  can  be 
pretty  limiting.   Physics  is  a  much  bigger  world,  and  certainly 
astronomy  is  too. 

When  Charlie  took  me  to  meet  Henry  and  I,  of  course,  had 
never  heard  of  the  ichneumonid  wasp,  trying  to  relate  to  Henry 
was  difficult.   I  felt  that  he  always  wondered  why  Charlie 
married  this  nincompoop  who  didn't  know  anything  about  insects. 
Then  when  Linda  was  married,  she  was  married  in  Ann  Arbor, 
because  we  had  moved  to  Berkeley  and  she  didn't  know  anybody 
here .   She  married  a  man  who  was  finishing  his  doctorate  there , 
as  she  was.   Henry  and  Marjorie  were  in  Ann  Arbor.   He  was 
teaching  at  the  University  of  Michigan,  and  they  said,  "We  will 
have  a  dinner  for  you  the  night  before  the  wedding,"  which  was 
wonderful . 

By  then  I  had  become  a  natural  science  decent,  and  I  was 
out  in  the  yard  helping  Henry  clean  it  up,  and  we  were  talking, 
and  I  thought,  "Now  I  know  something  about  insects,  so  I  will 
impress  Henry  with  my  knowledge."   I  started  talking  about 
insects ,  and  thanked  him  for  having  sent  the  wasp  to  the 
Oakland  Museum,  and  said,  "We  have  this  very  interesting  case 


654 


where  there  are  all  these  insects  related  to  the  High  Sierra, 
and  there's  this  very  interesting  insect  called  a 
grylloblattid." 

Henry  looked  up  at  me  and  said,  "Do  you  know  who 
discovered  the  first  grylloblattid  in  California?"  Guess  who? 
Henry  Townes.   He,  and  Charlie's  younger  brother,  George.   It's 
called  grylloblattid  bi-fraternilecta.   "By  two  brothers."   I 
felt  so- -here  I  thought  the  one  thing  that  I  knew  was  about  the 
grylloblattid  [laughs],  and  Henry  had  discovered  the  first 
grylloblattid  in  California! 

Henry  and  I  actually  got  to  like  each  other  through  the 
years.   I  think  he  realized  that  I  did  know  a  little  something 
after  all. 

Riess:      He  was  probably  quite  pleased. 

F.  Townes:   He  was  probably  pleased,  but  I  thought  it  was  the  irony  of  all 
ironies  that  I  would  try  to  impress  him  with  my  knowledge  of 
insects  and  choose  the  grylloblattid. 

So  that  [natural  science  work]  became  a  very  important 
thing.   But  then  twenty  years  is  enough  in  anything,  and  my  hip 
began  to  hurt  a  lot. 

Riess:      Now  how  is  it  that  in  that  case  you  ended  up  being  ensnared  in 
the  politics  of  the  situation  of  the  Oakland  Museum? 

F.  Townes:   Well,  I  didn't  really  get  very  ensnared.   I  was  on  the  board, 
which  wasn't  very  effective.   I  was  the  first  decent  asked  to 
join  the  women's  board.   Betty  Hine  called  me  when  I  was  decent 
council  president  and  said,  "We'd  like  to  invite  you  on  the 
women's  board."  And  I  said,  "Betty,  I  can't  do  it,  I'm  too 
busy."   "Well,  you'll  never  be  invited  again."   [laughs]   She 
finally  called  me  back  and  said,  "Would  you  join,  and  you 
wouldn't  have  to  do  anything  this  year." 

I  said,  "All  right.   I  would  like  to  be  able  to  come  on, 
because  I  would  like  the  women's  board  to  know  more  about  what 
the  docents  are  doing."   So  I  started  that  bridge  between  the 
women's  board  and  the  docents.   Then  they  invited  Gladys  Eaton, 
and  they  invited  Deborah  Wentworth,  and  they  got  some  really 
good  docents  in  there. 

Part  of  every  board  meeting  now  is  a  report  on  what  the 
docents  are  doing.   And  they  [the  women's  board]  began  to 
understand  what  they  were  selling  junk  [White  Elephant  sales] 
to  support.   So  I  got  into  that  part  of  the  museum,  but  I 


655 


didn't  really  get  involved  with  the  politics.   Like  Charlie,  I 
love  to  be  an  innovator,  it's  a  lot  of  fun,  but  I  got  fed  up 
with  the  constant  city  [of  Oakland]  versus  culture,  and  the 
women's  board  versus  the  docents,  et  cetera.   I  just  got  enough 
of  that.   But  that's  what  life  is,  I'm  afraid. 


University  Art  Museum  and  University  Extension  Jobs 


Riess:      Was  the  involvement  with  the  church,  then,  a  transition  from 
the  museum  to  homeless  work? 

F.  Townes:   It  was  a  transition  from  that,  and  I  know  Foster  is  asking  me 
this  in  my  times  with  him:  "How  did  you  get  called,  if  that's 
what  you  want  to  call  this,  to  do  this  ministry  with  the 
homeless?"   It  kind  of  happened.   How  can  I  say?   I  tried  to 
get  docenting  into  the  art  museum,  and  it  never  took.   I 
started  the  desk  there,  however. 

Riess:      At  the  University  Art  Museum? 

F.  Townes:   Yes,  I  started  the  desk.   There  had  been  no  contact  with  the 

public.   I  sat  at  the  desk  for  ages- -I  got  it  organized- -it  was 
the  only  contact  with  the  public. 

One  day  I  was  there,  and  Rachel  [Clark]  came  from 
[University]  Extension  and  said,  "I'm  looking  for  somebody  to 
help  me  in  the  office.   We're  starting  a  new  office,  and  trying 
to  get  more  people  interested  in  the  Extension  by  counseling 
them  and  [helping  them]  to  see  if  there's  a  way  to  get  back 
into  school  or  get  a  job  especially  through  Extension." 

Riess:      For  women? 

F.  Townes:   For  women  or  for  whomever.   And  I  said,  "Well,  what's  in  it  for 
such  a  person?"   She  said,  "You  can  take  courses  free."  And  I 
said,  "What  about  me?"   So  I  went  and  worked  for  her  one  day  a 
week  for  five  years . 

Riess:      How  did  you  make  the  counseling  known? 

F.  Townes:   Well,  when  people  applied  [enrolled  in  classes]  they  would 
often  be  sent  to  us,  and  of  course  word  of  mouth.   We  had  a 
little  office  on  the  ground  floor.   Rachel's  still  there.   She 
smokes  all  the  time,  and  everyone  came  down  and  smoked,  and  I 
almost  choked  to  death. 


656 


I  came  to  have  almost  a  specialty  with  older  women, 
because  so  many  of  my  women  friends  were  being  left,  and  would 
come  in  looking  for  new  directions ,  and  I  was  able  to  find  ways 
for  them  to  get  on  their  feet  by  going  back  to  school  as  a  way 
to  get  a  job. 

1  never  was  paid,  but  it  was  at  that  time  that  1  took 
quite  a  few  courses  at  Extension  for  free.   That's  when  I  took 
the  course  in  math  blocks.   And  in  the  years  1  was  with 
Extension  I  also  served  on  the  board  of  UREP  [University 
Research  Expeditions]  and  the  first  KQED  Community  Advisory 
Board. 


The  Chaplaincy  for  the  Homeless:  First  Congregational  Church. 
Berkeley 


F.  Townes:   Then  I  took  on  very  different  jobs  at  the  church. 
Riess:      The  church  is  the  Congregational  Church. 

F.  Townes:   First  Congregational.   It's  a  long  story,  for  eight  years,  with 
a  gap  about  three  years  ago  when  Bill  asked  me  to  work  on  arts 
for  the  church,  and  support  a  liturgical  artist  named  Nancy 
Chinn,  a  very  mixed  experience.   But  for  eight  years  I've  been 
running  the  adult  education  program  there . 

The  new  minister  came- -I  had  done  budget  for  the  former 
minister,  I'd  done  almost  everything  else,  too--and  Bill  (the 
new  minister)  saw  something  in  me  and  asked  me  to  do  adult 
education. 


Riess:      Bill  who? 

F.  Townes:   Bill  Gregory,  the  minister  who  has  now  left.   He  was  there  for 
seven  and  a  half  years.   I  knew  a  lot  of  people  on  campus,  and 
I  was  a  good  organizer  and  I  really  developed  an  interesting 
program. 

Riess:      What  was  the  name  of  the  position? 

F.  Townes:   I  still  have  the  same  half-time  position,  that  of  lay  associate 
for  adult  education  at  the  church.   Among  other  things,  I  do 
something  called  the  Second  Hour,  an  hour  after  the  worship 
service.   This  week  we  have  this  very  interesting  man  who  has 
written  a  book  on  the  life  and  death  of  Oscar  Romero  and  what 


657 


it  meant  to  El  Salvador.   We've  done  a  whole  lot  of  series, 
like  world  religions.   We  did  a  series  on  homelessness  when  it 
began  to  be  impacting  us. 

Riess:      And  you  call  upon  University  friends  and  people  to  do  programs? 

F.  Townes:  Yes,  all  for  free.   For  five  years  I  had  a  luncheon  at  The 
Faculty  Club  once  a  month  where  people  connected  with  the 
University  or  on  Holy  Hill  would  come  for  lunch.  Authorities 
would  come  and  talk  on  different  subjects,  for  example 
different  kinds  of  ethics,  and  I  would  introduce  them.   I  ran 
out  of  people  after  a  while  [laughs],  friends  and  people.   But 
I  think  I  have  brought  a  lot  of  enrichment  to  the  church. 

All  the  time  these  homeless  people  were  around,  and  I  had 
a  friend,  Rowena  Jackson,  who-  -I  don't  know  how  this  happened-  - 
but  she  suggested  I  go  and  work  for  something  called  the  Help 
Desk,  a  volunteer  job  which  was  at  the  BOSS  office,  Berkeley- 
Oakland  Support  Services.   I  worked  there  for  two  years,  and 
homeless  people  came  in  all  the  time.   The  desk  was  run  by  a 
few  churches  with  a  little  money  and  it  was  such  a  band-  aid 
effort. 


Townes:     We'd  give  them  a  bus  ticket  or  send  them  to  a  transitional 
house  for  a  few  nights.   I  just  saw  that  that  was  not  going 
anywhere  . 

Riess:      This  was  six  years  ago? 

F.  Townes:   Yes.   About  six  years  ago.   And  then  I  went  at  night  to  the 

drop-  in  center  run  by  the  Berkeley  Emergency  Food  Project,  and 
I  found  that  I  was  relating  to  these  people  and  beginning  to 
see  some  of  the  needs.   Anyway,  when  they  [homeless]  were 
impacting  our  cloister-  -seventy-  five  a  night,  defecating  and 
all  --it  was  terribly  hard  on  my  church,  and  somebody  said  to 
me,  "Would  you  get  a  committee  together  and  see  if  there's  any 
alternative?  Otherwise,  we  have  to  throw  them  out." 


Well,  I  got  a  group  together,  and  then  it  expanded  into 
fifteen  churches,  and  that's  how  it  got  started,  the  Berkeley 
Ecumenical  Chaplaincy  to  the  Homeless,  BECH.   But  it  was  one 
thing  leading  to  another,  and  now  I'm  really  struggling  to  keep 
it  going  financially.   I'm  using  every  talent  I  have.   And  in  a 
half  an  hour  I'm  going  to  go  over  and  talk  about  taking  over 
the  volunteers  for  the  Chaplaincy. 


658 


Our  innovation  is  to  train  some  homeless  people  to  be 
extensions  of  the  staff  to  help  other  homeless  people.   Through 
this  they  not  only  re-build  their  self  image,  but  they 
hopefully  can  help  other  homeless  people  get  back  into  society. 
This  is  the  peer  chaplain  idea,  which  is  now  in  four  or  five 
cities  all  over  the  country. 

We  do  these  educationals  on  "What  is  homelessness?"  and 
how  we  work  with  it,  and  about  the  programs  we  have  developed. 
I  talked,  because  Bonnie  couldn't  do  it,  two  weeks  ago  to  a 
sociology  class  at  Cal  who  asked  us  to  do  an  educational.   And 
then  we  sent  five  of  our  peer  chaplains  with  the  group 
[sociology  class]  into  the  streets,  and  they  did  what  we  call  a 
"reality  tour."  They  took  them  to  see  the  homeless  situation 
and  meet  the  homeless  people. 

Then  we've,  of  course,  been  involved  with  the  political 
scene,  although  I've  backed  off  from  that  a  lot  because  I'm  fed 
up  with  politics  here.   They're  about  to  cancel  all  money  for 
homelessness  in  Berkeley  to  balance  the  budget!   It's  just  a 
mess.   I  would  go  to  City  Hall--. 

But  anyway,  our  new  chaplain  is  much  more  spiritually- 
oriented,  as  I  am,  and  less  politically-oriented  than  her 
predecessor  was.   So  now  we've  decided  that  I  should  take  on 
the  volunteers.   We  have  volunteers,  we  have  staff,  we  have 
board  members  made  up  of  representatives  from  fifteen  churches, 
and  we  have  homeless.   We  meet  once  a  month,  all  these  groups, 
in  a  council  where  we  vote  on  policy  issues. 

Just  this  week  we  did  the  most  remarkable  thing.   We 
passed  some  rules  that  the  peer  chaplains  in  their  own  meetings 
had  already  decided  on,  that  violence  was  one  of  the  biggest 
problems  they  had  and  there  had  to  be  constraints  on  violence: 
sexual  violence,  verbal  harassment,  physical  violence,  all 
different  aspects.  They  brought  this  to  the  bigger  community, 
the  council,  and  it  was  almost  turned  down  the  first  time  by, 
actually,  one  of  the  representatives  from  Newman  Hall,  because 
they  thought  that  we  weren't  being  kind  enough.   But  we  passed 
it  finally. 

Now  people  will  be  rejected  from  our  community,  which  has 
become  a  family- -we're  the  support  family  for  these  homeless 
people  who  don't  have  families,  we're  their  family- -it's  the 
punishment  of  exclusion,  if  they  are  violent.   There  are  very 
definite  ways  of  measuring  violence,  and  we  reject  them  for  a 
day,  and  then  for  two  weeks,  and  then  for  six  months.   And  if 
they  don't  get  professional  help,  which  we  will  help  them  find, 
then  they  are  indefinitely  suspended.   It's  taken  four  years  to 


659 


get  to  this  point.   It  didn't  come  from  the  staff  and  the  board 
and  the  volunteers.   It  came  from  the  homeless  people 
themselves.   Now  that's  a  triumph,  isn't  it? 

Riess:      It  is.   Yes. 

F.  Townes:   And  these  people  are  going  back  to  school  and  getting  jobs. 

And  about  a  third  of  them  are  making  it.   But  it's  a  long  haul, 
and  we  cannot  help  one  thousand  people  out  there. 

Riess:      It  sounds  like  you're  very  high  on  patience  and  creativity. 

F.  Townes:   Yes,  and  I  am  stubborn  too.   I  will  not  give  up.   I  can  tell 
you  that  my  acceptance  by  "those  people,"  if  that's  what  you 
want  to  call  them,  as  a  person  for  whom  they  have  a  lot  of 
love,  well,  I'm  that  rich  bitch  who  lives  in  North  Berkeley  in 
a  big  house.   They've  all  been  here  many  times.   I  represent 
everything  that's  inimicable  to  them.   Successful  husband, 
intellectual  values,  everything.   But  they  have  real  love  for 
me,  and  I  for  them,  and  it's  the  most  accepted  that  I've  ever 
felt  in  my  life. 

Yesterday,  after  I  went  to  the  early  communion  service  at 
my  church  and  we  had  this  Oscar  Romero  person,  then  I  came 
home,  changed  my  clothes,  and  went  to  People's  Park- -we  hold  a 
service  there  every  Sunday- -where  we  had  a  service  on  the 
prodigal  son.   [laughs]   I  heard  the  prodigal  son  in  three 
different  versions  that  day. 

[To  them]  I  was  the  older  brother,  the  pharisee,  all  those 
things.   They  all  talked  about  how  they'd  been  rejected  by 
their  families,  by  society.   And  then  I  said,  "Yes,  I'm  the 
older  brother."  They  all  looked  at  me  and  I  said,  "I'm  the 
person  who  says,  'I've  always  followed  the  right  thing.   God, 
why  don't  you  recognize  me?'"  And  what  did  God  do?   He 
recognized  the  prodigal  son,  the  sinner. 

We  had  a  wonderful  time.   Bonnie  was  there  leading  it,  and 
she  had  me  read  the  parable.   And  they  listened,  and  we  hugged 
each  other.   It  is  just  a  very  special  time,  when  these  people 
join  our  circle.   The  University  lets  us  do  that.   We  don't 
have  any  crosses,  it's  not  a  service  in  the  real  sense,  but  we 
have  a  spirituality  committee  of  chaplains  that  meets  every 
week  and  reads  the  lectionary  and  decides  what  the  theme  will 
be  on  Sunday.   Bonnie  takes  that  and  develops  some  kind  of 
sermonizing  or  idea  around  it,  and  a  service.   I  just  wander  in 
when  I  can. 


660 


A  Lifetime's  Commitment  to  the  Common  Good 


F.  Tovnes:   What  I  can  say  is  that  "commitment  to  the  common  good  in  the 

face  of  discouragement,  ambiguity,  and  the  awareness  of  global 
complexity" --this  is  a  non- solvable  problem.   Especially  in  the 
light  of  our  present  uninspired  people  running  for  the 
presidency,  with  beknighted  attitudes  about  this  country  right 
now.   I  am  very  deeply  depressed  about  our  attitudes  and  about 
the  world  right  now.   I  think  we're  in  a  sorry  state.   That's  a 
nice  note  to  end  on  isn't  it?   [laughter] 

Charlie  is  never  pessimistic.   I  have  to  end  by  saying 
that  he  is  the  world's  true  optimist.   And  I  must  be  too, 
actually,  although  I  get  discouraged.   And  I  know  that  I'm  only 
going  to  live  a  finite  time,  and  I  want  to  live  it  as  fully  as 
I  can.   Charlie  knows  that  his  work  may  not  be  finished- -there 
is  no  one  right  now  to  bear  the  torch.  He  says,  "If  my  work  is 
valuable,  somebody  will  come  along  and  take  it  up.   And  if  it 
isn't,  it  will  fail." 

I  have  the  same  feeling.   I  can  push  the  Chaplaincy  only 
so  far,  and  if  we  can't  support  it,  and  it  closes,  I  will  have 
done  the  best  I  could.   That's  the  way  you  have  to  live.   As 
long  as  the  work  you're  doing  is  moving  ahead  and  satisfying 
for  you  and  you  are  growing,  you  can  do  it. 

Riess:      Yes,  I  guess  the  important  way  to  judge  is  whether  it's  a 
satisfying  and  growing  experience  for  you. 

F.  Townes:  And  the  way  that  I  judge  it  for  me  is  that  I  can  go  down  to  the 
museum  and  decent ,  or  I  can  go  to  France  and  speak  French  to 
Mitterand,  and  I  can  relate  really  quite  well  to  my  children, 
and  I  can  entertain  Charlie's  friends  and  not  put  my  foot  in  my 
mouth  too  much.   [laughter]   And  now  I  can  also  relate  to  "that 
bum"  in  the  street  and  find  the  godlike  spark  in  that  person. 

Riess:      A  lot  of  people  would  find  that  the  most  mysterious  of  the 
tasks  that  you  do . 

F.  Townes:   If  you  could  see  Michael- -he' s  back  at  school  now- -if  you  could 
have  seen  him  before.   You  know,  like  Better  Homes  and  Gardens' 
"before  and  after."  I  wish  I  had  a  picture  of  him  then, 
scraggly,  dirty,  he  didn't  care  about  himself.   He's  back  at 
school,  he's  learned  to  spell,  he's  going  to  get  an  A.B. 
degree.   He's  healthy,  well-dressed,  and  clean. 

Christ  says,  "To  the  least  of  these--."   If  we  did  it  for 
only  one  person  we  ought  to  be  glad.   But  we're  doing  it  for 


661 


Riess: 

F.  Townes: 


more  than  one.   Certainly  when  you  go  out  to  get  money  you  have 
to  say  there's  more  than  one  person  that  you've  been  able  to 
help  and  that  is,  of  course,  true.   It's  really  exciting. 

That's  a  much  better  note  to  end  on. 
Yes,  I  think  so.   [laughter] 


Addendum  fin  resconse  to  letter  of  June  24.  19921 


Riess:      Frances,  after  you  told  me  about  the  evening  reminiscing  with 

the  Walter  Gordon  family  member  I  got  to  thinking  that  that  was 
a  gem  of  oral  history  that  might  somehow  be  incorporated  in 
your  memoir.   I  wonder  if  you  would  sit  down  and  write  it  as 
you  told  it  to  me  on  the  telephone.   I  would  indicate  that  it 
was  an  afterthought,  the  result  of  a  later  conversation. 

F.  Townes:   Tonight  Edwin  Gordon,  historian,  teacher  and  graduate  student, 
the  great -great -grandson  of  a  slave  named  Jim  Gordon,  is  having 
dessert  and  coffee  with  us.   Edwin  turns  out  to  be  a  neighbor. 
He's  part  of  the  famous  Walter  Gordon  family- -Walter  Gordon, 
the  Cal  grad  and  Cal  football  player  who  became  a  lawyer  and 
was  governor  of  the  Virgin  Islands. 

I  was  named  for  General  John  B.  Gordon's  wife,  Fanny 
Haarlson,  who  was  married  at  sixteen  on  the  family  plantation 
in  Atlanta  to  General  Gordon.   Jim  was  married  on  the  same 
plantation  at  the  same  time  to  Sarah,  who  was  my  great - 
grandmother's  ladies'  maid.   Jim  was  manservant  to  the  General. 
"Feedama,"  the  name  I  gave  my  great  grandmother  (as  I  couldn't 
say  "Sweet  Grandma"  when  I  was  a  little  girl)  spent  the  last 
years  of  her  life- -she  was  in  her  nineties- -in  northern  New 
Hampshire  where  I  grew  up.   General  Gordon  was  the  youngest 
general  of  the  Confederacy  during  the  Civil  War,  and  later 
governor  and  senator. 

Jim  and  Sarah  were  freed  at  the  end  of  the  war  and  my 
great-grandfather  helped  their  children  get  higher  education. 
Some  of  them  moved  to  Texas . 

At  a  time  when  race  tension  persists  in  our  country  I  feel 
privileged  to  be  connected  to  a  distinguished  black  family.   My 
brother  met  Walter  Gordon  in  the  Virgin  Islands  when  Walter  was 
governor  and  my  brother  was  assistant  secretary  of  labor.   I 
met  Walter  Gordon's  widow  when  I  moved  here  twenty- five  years 
ago,  and  now  I  know  Edwin. 


662
