Appendix  A 

Talk  Given  at  Jason  Group  Twenty  Fifth  Anniversary  by  Charles  Townes , 

November  30,  1984 


Well,  thank  you.   It  is  a  pleasure  to  think  back  over  some  of  the 
struggles  and  the  successes  of  the  past.   1  find  it  much  more  difficult  to 
give  the  first  part  of  my  talk.   Bill  [William  Aaron]  Nierenberg  asked  me 
to  talk  and  make  some  general  remarks  about  the  problem  of  advice  to  the 
government,  and  I  am  very  hesitant  to  do  that  because  there  are  so  many 
experts  here  and  the  problem  is  really  a  very  complex  one.   Nevertheless, 
to  try  to  fulfill  the  assignment,  I  am  going  to  ruminate  some  about  this 
field,  and  then  talk  about  the  early  history  of  Jason. 

In  1951,  Bertrand  Russell  said,  "We  are  only  at  the  very  beginnings 
of  the  work  of  science  in  transforming  human  life."  That  is  a  reflection 
of  the  position  that  I  suspect  most  of  us  believe,  that  the  relation 
between  science  and  technology  and  society,  and  policy  planning  for  that 
relationship,  is  really  terribly  important.   And  I  suspect  most  of  us  would 
agree  that  in  the  practice  of  the  politicians  and  government  administrators 
generally,  science  advice  is  not  used  enough.   On  the  other  hand,  I  suspect 
it  is  probably  true,  also,  that  in  the  eyes  of  scientists,  science  advice 
is  not  as  important  as  they  think.   Some  of  the  reason  for  that  is  not 
because  the  advice  itself  is  not  important,  but  there  is  a  diffusion  of 
scientific  information  and  policy  advice  that  comes  in  a  variety  of  ways, 
and  through  that  our  political  process  gets  educated,  as  well  as  by  more 
formal  processes. 

Many  different  kinds  of  systems  work.   You  might  consider,  for 
example,  the  Soviet  Union.   I  am  convinced  in  the  Soviet  Union  the  Soviet 
scientists  have  even  less  effect  on  policy  than  we  do  in  this  country;  they 
are  really  quite  removed  from  the  high  political  forces.   Not  that  they 
don't  play  a  role,  but  it  is  a  relatively  small  influence  on  policy  itself, 
and  the  primary  person  relied  on  for  science  policy  in  the  Soviet  Union, 
Alexandrov,  the  president  of  the  academy,  while  a  very  intelligent  man,  is, 
after  all,  eighty- two  years  old.  He  has  never  traveled  outside  the  Soviet 
Union,  he  doesn't  travel  on  a  plane- -he  goes  only  by  train- -and  very  likely 
will  be  elected  for  another  five-year  term.   [laughter]   I  understand  he 
goes  on  long  vacations.  Yet  the  Soviet  Union  is  successful  enough  that  we 
can  be  both  pleased  by  their  success  and  also  somewhat  worried. 

There  are  so  many  facets  to  scientific  advice,  it  is  really  very 
difficult  to  discuss  it  except  on  a  specific  basis.   I  think  one  of  the 
things  that  always  comes  in  focus  is  science  advice  to  the  president,  and  I 
would  refer  you  to  this  book,  edited  by  Bill  [William  T.]  Golden,  Science 
Advice  to  the  President  [Technology  in  Science,  Vol.  2,  Nos .  1,2,  1980, 
Pergamon  Press].   A  number  of  people  you  know  very  well  have  written 
articles  in  here,  Guy  [H.  Guyford]  Stever,  Jerry  [Jerome  B.]  Wiesner,  Dick 
[Richard  L. ]  Garwin.   So  they  wrote  the  book,  and  I  think  you  will  find  it 


666 


really  quite  an  interesting  discussion  of  the  history  and  context  and 
meaning  of  science  advice  to  the  president. 

Now,  there  are  different  types  of  needs.   For  example,  certain  needs 
are  simply  to  get  the  right  answer  to  a  technical  question.   That  is  one 
kind  of  science  advice,  relatively  straightforward  in  a  sense,  if  you  can 
really  get  the  right  answer.   There,  science  and  technology  are  obviously 
intimately  involved,  and  few  administrators  hesitate  [to  get  science 
advice]  when  there  is  a  real  scientific  question  that  is  soluble  in  terms 
of  numbers  or  something  like  this,  and  will  definitely  be  either  right  or 
wrong.   But  then  there  is  the  broader  question,  the  general  view  of  the 
future  and  the  planning  of  immediate  policies.   That  is  a  more  difficult 
and  more  touchy  one . 

There  is  also  the  function  of  window-dressing,  and  I  would  not 
downplay  the  function  of  window-dressing.   It  can  be  misused,  of  course, 
but  getting  the  confidence  of  the  public  that  things  are  being  adequately 
considered  is  quite  important,  and  one  shouldn't  despise  it.   1  think  the 
original  PSAC  [President's  Science  Advisory  Committee]  formed  by  Jim  [James 
R. ]  Killian  [Jr.],  under  Eisenhower,  really  played  a  big  part  in  that  kind 
of  function:  to  reassure  the  public  that  the  problems  are  being  looked  at. 
The  public  was  really  very  worried  at  that  time  as  a  result  of  Sputnik.   It 
was  very  worried.   It  had  been  subjected  to  a  psychological  shock. 
Eisenhower  tried  a  number  of  techniques  to  try  to  reassure  the  public,  and 
I  think  the  creation  of  PSAC  was  one  of  the  most  important  ones.   On  the 
other  hand,  of  course,  window-dressing,  if  it  is  meaningful,  must  have 
content  at  the  same  time . 

Another  function  is  just  to  obtain  a  consensus  among  scientists  who 
have  their  varying  opinions.  This  is  particularly  the  problem  of  NSF,  to 
get  a  consensus  of  what  to  do,  primarily  a  consensus  among  scientists. 

Next  there  is  the  view  from  outside,  which  is  very  important.   A  view 
from  the  outside  which  brings  into  administration  new  ideas,  criticisms--. 
It  can  bring  criticism  inside  the  family  and  provide  a  very  different  kind 
of  interaction  from  criticism  outside  the  family,  and  might  simply  just 
check  over  things,  examine  whether  something  is  really  wrong  with  their 
present  policies  or  present  aims. 

There  are  certain  kinds  of  conditions  which  must  be  met  in  order  for 
science  advice  to  be  very  effective.   First,  certainly,  it  must  be  a 
strongly  felt  need.   You  look  at  the  early  history  of  science  advice  in  the 
U.S. --not  so  early,  but  back  through  the  war  years --it  was  a  war  which 
really  brought  [Vannevar]  Bush  and  [James  B.]  Conant  into  a  prominent  place 
in  contact  with  Roosevelt.   It  was  a  kind  of  informal  advice,  in  a  way,  but 
yet  very  important.  As  a  result  of  that,  the  first  presidential  advisory 
group  was  formed.   This  was  not  PSAC  as  we  all  know  it.   Oliver  [E.] 
Buckley  headed  that  first  presidential  advisory  group,  and  probably  many  of 
you  never  heard  of  Oliver  Buckley- -he  was  the  president's  advisor  in  1951, 


667 


he  died  shortly  after.   He  was  president  of  Bell  Telephone  Laboratories  and 
I  happen  to  know  him  well,  but  otherwise  was  not  a  prominent  figure  in 
American  science. 

That  group  was  organized  as  a  result  of  Truman's  asking  someone  to 
look  into  what  might  be  done  in  strengthening  the  scientific  base  for 
national  defense.   He  asked  an  investment  specialist  from  Wall  Street,  Bill 
Golden.   Many  of  you  know  Bill  Golden;  he  has  become  very  close  to  science 
and  to  scientists.   He  was  asked  as,  I  say,  a  Wall  Street  man,  but  also  as 
a  kind  of  right  hand  aid  to  Admiral  [Lewis  L. ]  Strauss  at  the  time,  by 
Truman  to  consider  what  might  be  done.   He  consulted  many  scientists  and 
initiated  a  science  advisory  group.   They  didn't  do  a  great  deal,  but  one 
thing  they  did  was  to  found  the  NSF,  which  was  very  important.   It  was  a 
group  of  wise  men,  but  their  scope  and  what  they  could  do  at  that  time  was 
somewhat  limited.   In  fact,  they  changed  NSF  from  being  primarily  something 
oriented  toward  defense  science  as  its  primary  purpose,  to  something  which 
was  to  strengthen  science  generally  in  the  U.S.,  and  rather  specifically 
not  oriented  towards  defense. 

Another  result  of  that  strongly  felt  need  as  Sputnik  came  along, 
along  with  other  pressures,  was  of  course,  the  creation  of  PSAC  under  Jim 
Killian.   The  creation  of  ARPA  (and  ARPA  at  that  time,  when  it  was  created, 
was  started  as  a  new  service,  on  equal  footing  with  the  other  three 
services  when  it  was  founded) ,  again  to  try  to  give  the  public  confidence 
that  there  was  something  being  done  and  to  get  science  in  the  right 
context.   Jason  itself  was  a  result  of  that  kind  of  need. 

Another  aspect  which  is  propitious  for  beginning  of  a  new  system,  is 
the  start  of  a  new  administration.   Whenever  there  is  a  new  administrative 
team,  there  is  a  new  opening  to  do  something.   They  want  to  do  something, 
and  it  makes  an  occasion  for  reexamining  things.   I  might  remind  you  that 
President  Nixon,  just  before  he  came  into  office,  had  many,  many  committees 
studying  various  kinds  of  problems,  trying  to  work  out  policy.  He  was  very 
strong  on  getting  advice.   And  in  the  final  meeting  of  the  committee 
chairmen,  he  got  up  and  said,  "Well,  I  learned  there  are  more  Democrats 
here  than  Republicans"- -he  had  tapped  the  intellectual  community- -"and 
that's  just  the  way  it  should  be.   We  are  going  to  all  work  together,  and 
I'm  going  to  pull  in  the  Democrats  and  they  are  going  to  help,"  and  so  on. 
You  will  notice  that  is  somewhat  different  from  Nixon's  subsequent  career. 
As  he  became  more  established,  and  then  became  more  beleaguered,  there  was 
more  and  more  of  a  break,  and  he  didn't  follow  up  on  that  really  broad- 
scale  advice  from  the  community  very  much.   Nevertheless,  there  was  a 
really  promising  occasion,  and  there  usually  is  when  something  new  gets 
started  and  a  new  administration  comes  in. 

Now,  those  conditions  are  always  tempered,  of  course,  by  the 
proclivities  of  the  president  and  his  representatives  and  appointees,  by 
public  views,  and  by  various  kinds  of  symbolism.   I  think  our  government 
tends,  for  example,  to  swing  back  and  forth  with  time  between  emphasizing 


668 


different  kinds  of  groups  advising  government.   To  some  extent,  that  may  be 
linked  with  the  swing  back  and  forth  between  Republicans  and  Democrats ,  but 
I  don't  think  entirely.   It  is  really  basically  the  problem  that  no  system 
is  adequate  for  the  kind  of  ideals  that  we  all  have  for  government,  and  how 
government  should  perform,  and  no  government  really  fulfills  that.   There 
is  a  dissatisfaction—one  sees  the  failures  and  then  we  try  something 
different.   So  there  is  a  swing  back  and  forth  from  trusting  one  group  as 
being  the  saviors  of  society  maybe,  and  finding  that  it  hasn't  been  perfect 
and  another  group  is  called  in. 

If  you  just  look  at  that  situation,  Roosevelt  was  really  rather 
closely  tied  to  the  intellectual  community,  and  certainly  called  on 
intellectuals  more  than  most  other  presidents  have.   That  lasted  on  through 
Truman's  time  to  some  extent.   By  the  time  Eisenhower  came  in,  the  country 
was  a  bit  disillusioned  with  the  intellectual  community,  and  Eisenhower 
then  called  primarily  on  the  businessmen,  and  I  think  would  have  continued 
to  do  that  if  Sputnik  and  other  events  had  not  come  along,  and  the 
scientific  and  intellectual  community  might  have  been  relatively  uncalled- 
upon.   But  the  businessman  became  very  prominent  and  important  to 
Eisenhower  as  a  part  of  the  swing. 

When  Kennedy  came  in,  he  again  asked  the  help  of  the  intellectual 
community.   That  lasted  through  Johnson,  but  began  to  decay,  and  we  had 
this  curious  and  complicated  mixture  in  the  case  of  Nixon.   Now  we  have 
Reagan  who  really  counts  on  the  business  community  primarily  for  his 
advice.   There  is  a  business  council  which  meets  regularly  with  the 
president,  and  there  is  no  such  science  council.   I  think  one  has  to  expect 
that  kind  of  swing  back  and  forth. 

The  DOD  [Department  of  Defense],  of  course,  is  much  more  consistent. 
The  DOD  is  more  consistent  [for]  a  number  of  good  reasons.   One  is  that 
there  is  a  very  high  and  obvious  technical  content  to  the  problems  of  the 
DOD  that  one  just  can't  ignore.   Secondly,  there  is  a  very  strong  influence 
in  the  DOD  by  long  term  career  personnel  who  maintain  more  continuity,  more 
consistency,  and  so  the  DOD  is  more  consistent  and  more  planned  and  not 
quite  so  subject  to  these  strong  swings. 

Conditions  for  effectiveness  of  groups  like  this,  and  their  advice, 
one  might  also  list.   I  think  first,  there  are  certain  openings  at 
particular  times  that  are  matters  of  history,  when  there  is  a  chance  to  do 
something  new  and  make  a  new  start.   It  is  just  at  those  times  where  one 
has  to  plan  very  carefully  the  initiation  of  a  new  start,  plan  very 
carefully  because  the  situation  is  open,  we  don't  have  a  past  history  of 
maybe  some  mistakes  and  negative  feelings  and  people  being  locked  in. 

We  also  have  rather  positive  means  that  have  shown  up  which  create 
this  new  opportunity,  and  that  is  the  time  when  one  needs  to  work  out  the 
proper  formulation  and  the  charter,  and  there  is  a  chance  to  do  something. 
In  addition,  of  course,  there  must  be  a  function  for  the  advice  or  the 


669 


organization,  and  I  might,  as  an  example,  remind  you  of  the  re-creation  of 
something  like  PSAC  under  Ford.   PSAC  had  been  cancelled  by  Nixon.   When 
Ford  came  in,  there  was  a  national  recognition  that  something  had  to  be 
done. 

Clearly  the  nation  needed  science  advice,  and  Ford  was  an  earnest 
person  and  recognized  that,  and  so  he  created  a  new  council.   But  he  didn't 
know  what  to  do  with  it.   The  council,  furthermore,  was  generalized  to 
bring  in  the  businessmen,  and  Cap  [Caspar]  Weinberger  was  part  of  the 
science  advisory  team,  Si  [Simon]  Ramo  was  the  chairman  or  co-chairman. 
Ford  didn't  know  what  to  do  with  it.   He  knew  he  should  have  one,  but  he 
didn't  know  what  to  do  with  it,  and  in  fact  he  assigned  it  to  the  vice 
president  to  take  care  of,  which  is  another  indication  that  it  was  not 
exactly  functional.   It  had  a  few  meetings  and  then  decayed. 

There  must  also  be  a  supportive  chain  of  command,  that  is  critical 
for  the  success  of  this  kind  of  operation;  there  had  to  be  a  supportive 
chain  of  command,  and  not  just  in  the  first  echelon,  it  has  to  be  on  up  the 
line.   To  give  a  personal  example,  1  once  helped  form  a  committee  to  advise 
ACDA.   I  was  very  eager  to  do  that,  and  pleased  at  the  opportunity,  and  we 
formed  the  committee,  but  we  could  never  do  a  thing.   For  one  reason  or 
another,  anything  to  do  with  ACDA  that  was  important  seemed  to  be  beyond 
our  reach;  each  matter  was  too  confidential,  or  something  like  this,  to  be 
discussed  by  a  committee  yet.   And  we  never  got  to  do  a  thing. 

I  realized,  after  about  a  year,  what  the  problem  was:  that  Fred  Ikle 
had  wanted  this  committee,  and  he  was  head  of  ACDA,  but  we  had  on  the 
committee  a  couple  of  people  who  had  crossed  swords  with  Henry  Kissinger, 
and  Henry  Kissinger  was  a  past  master  at  seeing  that  nobody  with  the  wrong 
approaches  got  into  the  advisory  system.  There  was  just  no  hope  of  doing 
anything,  so  we  disbanded. 

There  was  another  case,  more  closely  connected  with  Jason,  where 
George  Kistiakowsky  resigned,  as  a  symbol  in  objection  to  some  of  the 
things  that  were  being  done  in  Vietnam,  from  being  chairman  of  the 
committee  which  was  in  charge  of  mines  and  detection  devices  which  Jason 
had  sponsored  in  order  to  try  to  build  a  sanitation  line  between  the  north 
and  the  south.   I  was  asked  to  undertake  that  assignment.   I  talked  with 
George  about  it,  and  George  felt  that  his  resignation  was  a  symbolic  thing, 
that  one  should  continue,  that  we  had  to  try  to  help,  and  it  was  reasonable 
for  me  to  undertake  it.   Paul  Nitze  was  undersecretary  then,  and  he  was 
very  supportive. 

On  the  other  hand,  in  this  case  the  lower  echelon  was  not.  There  was 
a  general  to  whom  we  reported,  really  a  gung-ho  general,  and  it  is  clear  he 
wanted  to  use  the  detectors  and  mines  in  a  completely  different  way.   He 
was  not  at  all  interested  in  the  things  that  the  Jason  people  had  been 
after.   I  have  forgotten  his  name,  but  he  was  a  general  who  was  later 
courtmartialed  for  making  an  organized  bombing  raid  on  North  Vietnam 


670 


against  the  orders  of  his  command.   He  was  a  smart  guy,  gung-ho,  great 
person,  but  he  just  had  a  different  orientation,  and  it  was  hard  for  us  to 
work  effectively.  Pretty  soon  we  decided  to  give  up  on  that  committee, 
too,  because  it  just  didn't  have  an  appropriate  match  to  the  people  up  the 
line. 

The  creation,  then,  of  a  successful  structure,  and  the  right 
combination,  is  a  product  that  may  be  in  some  cases  an  accidental  product 
of  a  particular  history.   1  think  we  have  to  recognize  that.   One  can't 
just  arbitrarily  say  we  ought  to  have  this  so  let's  get  it  organized. 

Jason,  of  course,  is  not  a  policy  body.   Frequently,  in  science 
advice,  we  think  of  policy  as  being  the  primary  thing.   Of  course,  it  is 
the  broadest  scope  of  all.   Jason  is  clearly  not  a  policy  body.   On  the 
other  hand,  it  is  clear  that  for  anything  we  do,  policy  is  just  around  the 
corner.   Any  choice  of  problem  involves  an  idea  of  policy.   What  problems 
we  work  on  involves  an  idea  of  policy,  what  assumptions  we  make  involves 
some  kind  of  idea  of  policy.   We  affect  policy,  in  some  cases  strongly.   We 
are  citizens,  and  we  are  concerned  about  policy.   Many  of  us  are  involved 
in  some  way  or  another  with  policy.   So,  while  one  cannot  disconnect  Jason 
from  policy,  nevertheless  it  is  not  a  policy  body.   It  is  clear,  I  think, 
that  we  need  a  broad- scale  body  dealing  with  science  and  technology  policy 
for  the  nation  as  a  whole.   1  don't  underrate  at  all  George  Keyworth's 
office  as  doing  a  very  important  and  very  successful  job.   Nevertheless,  it 
is  clearly  different  from  the  sort  of  broad  discussion  and  the  broad 
representation  which  PSAC  enjoyed  during  its  heyday. 

We  cannot  recreate  PSAC.   I  think  we  must  be  clear  about  that.   PSAC 
was  one  of  those  happy  accidents,  and  it  served  a  wonderful  purpose, 
particularly  in  its  heyday  during  the  Eisenhower  and  Kennedy 
administrations.   It  served  a  wonderful  purpose  and  very  well,  and  it  began 
to  be  more  and  more  difficult  as  time  went  on  to  be  effective.   It 
continued  to  be  somewhat  effective.   But  one  can't  recreate  that  situation 
and  there  are  also  these  swings. 

There  is  also,  I  think,  a  very  important  aspect  one  must  realize, 
that  the  exercise  of  influence  in  power  immediately  generates  some 
rebounds,  and  some  backlash.   That  can't  be  ignored.   The  fact  that 
scientists  had  such  an  important  role  for  a  long  time  was  not  looked  at  in 
a  happy  way  by  some  people  who  said,  "After  all,  scientists  are  pretty 
specialized  and  we  are  afraid  that  they  are  just  looking  after  their  own 
interests,"  and  so  on,  and  that  is  a  problem.   I  think  scientists  did  have, 
at  that  time,  a  very  special  kind  of  role,  and  I  believe  they  served  it 
very  well  and  served  the  nation  very  well.  On  the  other  hand,  as  I  said,  I 
don't  think  we  can  hope,  nor  perhaps  should  we  try,  to  recreate  PSAC. 

My  own  view  is  that  a  more  integrated  committee  would  be  best  if  it 
can  work.   A  more  integrated  committee  in  the  sense  of  bringing  in 
industrialists,  and  other  people  who  would  all  be  involved  in  science 


671 


policy.  Certainly  scientists,  but  not  scientists  alone.  The  difficulty 
with  that  is  that  scientists  don't  talk  easily  with  such  people  nor  vice 
versa.  And  getting  a  consensus  and  really  coming  to  grips  with  problems  in 
that  kind  of  a  group  is  not  easy  at  all.   Furthermore,  there  is  a  problem 
of  work.   If  a  policy  committee  is  going  to  be  successful,  it  has  got  to 
work  hard.   Academic  people  frequently  can  free  up  the  time  and  do  that, 
and  do,  but  it  is  much  more  difficult  for  businessmen  and  other  people 
apparently.   They  are  simply  not  in  the  habit  of  approaching  things  that 
way.   It  is  one  thing  to  feel  that  you  are  wise  and  you  can  give  broad 
advice,  but  lots  of  other  people  can  do  that  too.   The  additional  hard  work 
is  a  very  important  part  of  the  job. 

I  don't  think  it  is  easy  to  say  exactly  what  should  be  done. 
Nevertheless,  we  do  have  this  anomaly  at  the  moment:  there  is  a  business 
council  and  it  plays,  I  think,  a  relatively  important  function.   Science 
advice  tends  to  be  rather  more  piecemeal,  and  yet  to  the  nation  it  is 
obviously  important,  and  I  think  we  should  all  be  thinking  about  what 
should  be  done,  and  at  some  point  there  will  be  a  somewhat  modified 
structure.   Again,  let  me  say  I  don't  underplay  at  all  the  importance  of 
Keyworth's  office  and  what  he  is  doing.   I  think  he  is  doing  a  very 
important  and  effective  job,  but  it  is  a  somewhat  different  role. 

Now,  let  me  mention  a  few  of  the  problems,  a  few  of  the  exacerbating 
problems  in  this  kind  of  operation.   One  very  basic  problem  is  the  control 
of  the  advice  that  is  being  given.   Within  the  government  there  are,  of 
course,  many  good  people,  and  we  want  excellent  people  within  the 
government  who  can  themselves  give  the  right  kind  of  advice ;  we  want  to 
encourage  that.   There  is  a  natural  problem  between  the  inside  advice, 
working  at  it  full  time,  feeling  they  have  an  intimate  stake  in  it,  and 
outside  advice  which  tends  to  be  part  time  or  at  least  not  controlled  by 
the  administrators  who  have  to  work  out  and  activate  policy.   If  the 
outside  advice  is  antipathetical  to  the  policies  that  administrators  think 
really  ought  to  be  carried  out,  there  is  a  very  deep  problem. 

Let  me  give  you  the  example  of  WSEG.   WSEG  is  the  Weapons  Systems 
Evaluation  Group.   It  was  a  branch  of  IDA  at  one  time.   The  reason  I  happen 
to  be  personally  familiar  with  it- -and  let  me  say  I  apologize  for 
commenting  particularly  on  the  things  I  was  personally  involved  in;  the 
reason  I  do  it  is  I  can  be  surest  that  I  might  be  right  and  might  remember 
the  situations  correctly.   WSEG  was  a  distinguished  group.   It  was  at  one 
time  headed,  or  at  least  had  the  services  of  Bright  Wilson,  for  example, 
Bill  [William]  Shockley,  then  H.  P.  Robertson,  and  was,  at  that  time,  a 
relatively  small  group.   It  grew  in  size,  and  IDA  undertook  to  manage  it, 
IDA  being  a  nonprofit  organization  run  by  a  group  of  universities 
specifically  to  try  to  do  this  kind  of  thing  for  government. 

The  idea  was  to  provide  an  independent  assessment  of  the 
effectiveness  of  different  weapons  systems.   The  group  worked  within  the 
Pentagon- -they  were  employees  of  IDA  and  we  presumably  supervised  their 


672 


work  and  insured  that  they  had  the  right  kind  of  freedom  and  could  say  what 
they  wanted  to  say.   But  that  was  pretty  objectionable  in  some  cases, 
because  sometimes  they  didn't  come  out  with  the  "right"  answers,  or  at 
least  the  answers  that  the  people  in  the  Pentagon  thought  would  really  be 
best  or  appropriate  or  support  what  they  were  trying  to  do,  and  what  they 
were  trying  to  do  was  important.   And  many  of  these  answers  are  debatable, 
one  has  to  recognize.   They  depend  on  particular  assumptions,  and  they  are 
debatable . 

How  to  maintain  an  independent  outside  view  with  something  as 
intimately  connected  with  the  Pentagon  as  which  weapons  you  choose  in 
detail,  and  what  their  effectiveness  is,  that  is  a  deep  problem.   It 
lasted,  I  think,  very  well  for  some  time.  But  eventually  there  was  a 
clash.  The  administrators  in  the  Pentagon  felt  that  they  needed  to  control 
the  people  and  make  a  final  approval  of  the  product  and  what  was  being 
advised,  not  an  independent  outside  group;  that  they  needed  to  see  what 
these  reports  actually  finally  said,  and  supervise  them.   Not  that  they 
were  going  to  try  to  change  somebody's  mind,  but  they  had  to  do  the  editing 
and  the  discussion  about  exactly  what  was  said.   They  felt  the  people  then 
reporting,  supposedly,  their  salaries  paid  by  IDA,  were  really  employees  of 
the  Pentagon  and  had  no  freedom.   At  that  point,  the  system  had  to  break 
down,  of  course,  and  these  people  then  worked  for  a  while  in  a  different 
context,  and  eventually  WSEG  crumbled  away. 

But  that  is  the  nature  of  the  problem:  to  maintain  this  dichotomy  of 
an  outside  view,  and  an  independent  view  that  is  very  carefully  cultured  to 
be  independent,  and  at  the  same  time  a  kind  of  responsibility  to  the  people 
who  have  to  carry  out  policy,  and  a  continuity  in  their  policy,  with  all  of 
the  tugging  and  hauling  that  is  involved  in  any  real  choices.   Of  course, 
there  must  be  inside  people,  and  of  course  they  have  a  say,  too,  in  giving 
advice,  but  I  think  the  outside  view  is  generally  quite  important,  and  is 
something  we  must  struggle  to  maintain  in  the  face  of  this  kind  of  anomaly 
and  possible  inconsistency  with  how  it  is  actually  practical  to  do  things. 

A  second  kind  of  problem  I  want  particularly  to  mention  is  that  of 
inside  advice  versus,  let's  say,  outside  advice  and  public  influence.   That 
is  an  everlasting  problem  when  advisors  work  inside  of  government, 
particularly  obtaining  confidential  information,  not  just  necessarily 
classified  information,  but  when  an  administrator  lets  down  his  hair  to  a 
group  of  people  and  says,  "Look,  this  is  my  problem,  and  we've  got  to  do 
something.   What  would  you  advise?"  That  is  important,  that  he  let  down 
his  hair,  informally,  and  be  willing  to  talk  intimately.   But  it  is  also 
important  that  he  knows  that  those  people  are  going  to  be  trying  to  help 
him- -at  least  help  him  do  a  good  job.   The  problem  is  that  these  people  are 
also  citizens.   They  have  a  responsibility  at  large  and  they  are  bound  to 
be  hounded  by  newsmen,  asked  by  congress  their  opinions,  and  they  should 
have  a  right  to  speak.   The  outside  contact  is  an  important  one,  too.   And 
the  balance  between  those  two  is,  I  think,  the  other  dichotomy  that  one  has 
to  look  at  very  carefully. 


673 


It's  frequently  said  that  PSAC  was  basically  killed  by  two  or  three 
events  involving  public  attacks  on  the  policies  of  the  president  by  people 
involved  in  PSAC.   Now,  I  don't  give  that  complete  credibility,  though  I 
have  no  doubt  these  events  had  an  impact.   I  think  there  were  many  factors 
that  were  involved,  the  Vietnam  period  being  perhaps  the  critical  thing  and 
the  general  breakdown  of  trust  within  our  society,  segmentation  of  our 
society  in  different  camps,  and  so  on,  which  I  think  was  the  basic  problem. 

Nevertheless,  there  are  such  problems,  and  again  I  will  mention  one 
of  my  own  which  I  think  is  a  rather  minor  one,  just  as  an  illustration: 
There  was  a  time  during  Lee  DuBridge's  tenure  as  advisor  to  the  president 
when  Murray  Cell -Mann  and  1  were  on  PSAC  and  wanted  to  sign  a  public 
statement  that  was  going  to  be  published  in  the  New  York  Times,  and  it  was 
not  on  a  subject  which  PSAC  had  itself  discussed,  but  it  disagreed  somewhat 
with  White  House  policy.   We  were  very  careful  to  ask  Lee  DuBridge,  "Do  you 
think  we  should  be  signing  this  or  not?"  Lee,  being  a  man  of  principle, 
rather  than  of  politics,  said,  "Well,  yes,  I  think  it  is  all  right.   There 
is  no  reason  you  should  not  sign  it."  So  we  did.   I'm  convinced  that  also 
had  a  deleterious  affect.   Now,  one  might  wonder,  should  we  have  done  it  or 
shouldn't  we  have?  As  a  matter  of  fact,  I  can't  even  remember  exactly  what 
we  said.   [laughter]  And  maybe  the  effect  on  the  administrative  system  was 
much  more  important  than  our  effect  on  the  public.   I  don't  know,  but  that 
is  the  thing  one  has  to  debate . 

You  will  find  a  very  forthright  statement  in  here  by  Dick  Garwin  as 
to  principles  in  cases  like  that.   I  think  it  is  interesting,  and  the 
principles  mentioned  are  important.   But  there  is  more  than  principles 
involved.   We  thought  we  were  carrying  out  the  right  principles.   We  asked 
Lee  DuBridge,  and  it  was  Lee's  job  to  decide,  and  it  was  something  that  had 
not  been  discussed  in  PSAC.   Nevertheless,  I  am  not  sure  we  did  the  right 
thing,  and  I  don't  think  the  solutions  to  those  problems  are  easy.   I  don't 
think  principles  really  tell  you  an  answer.  You  have  to  look  at  the 
particular  case  and  see  what  you  are  doing.   Are  you  improving  the  system 
or  hurting  it,  and  at  what  costs,  and  so  on?  That  is  an  eternal  problem 
that  all  of  us  have  to  face,  and  certainly  those  of  us  in  Jason,  but  also 
in  any  advisory  body. 

Closely  connected  with  that  is  a  question  of  trust.  Here,  I  think, 
friendship  is  an  unusually  potent  influence.   I  remember  very  well  when 
George  Kistiakowsky  told  me,  "Well,  you  know  Eisenhower  is  beginning  to 
call  me  George,  and  that  is  terribly  important."   I  thought  this  was  maybe 
a  little  vanity  on  George's  part  that  it  made  any  difference  what  Ike  was 
calling  him,  but  I  realized  later  that  really  was  very  important,  that 
Eisenhower  felt  at  home  with  Kistiakowsky.   There  was  a  relationship  there 
of  trust  and  ease  of  discussion  and  so  on  which  is  very  important.   And 
personal  relations  and  personal  friendship  for  people  that  are  advising, 
and  a  sense  of  trust,  is  a  terribly  important  thing. 


674 


George  Miller,  who  headed  the  Apollo  program,  I  happened  to  know 
first,  by  chance,  as  a  young  microwave  spectroscopist.   That  was  sort  of 
his  first  work  out  of  school.   He  did  some  microwave  spectroscopy ,  so  I 
knew  him.   When  he  became  the  head  of  the  space  program,  I  ran  into  him  and 
I  said,  "George,  you  know,  you  get  a  great  deal  of  criticism  from  the 
scientific  community  as  to  what  the  Apollo  program  is  doing,  and  much  of  it 
is  valid.   What  you  ought  to  do  is  get  these  people  together,  who  are 
earnest  and  intelligent  people,  get  them  together  and  bring  them  into  your 
own  house  and  have  them  tell  you  their  criticisms,  and  work  with  you  and 
try  to  see  that  things  are  done  the  best  possible  way.   After  all,  this  is 
a  national  program,  it  is  going  to  go  through,  everybody  wants  it  to  work 
as  well  as  possible,  and  you  have  got  lots  of  critics  interested  in  it. 
Bring  them  in."  Naturally,  in  a  case  like  that,  he  got  me  involved  and  it 
was  one  of  the  most  interesting  committees  I  think  I  have  ever  been  on, 
because  many  of  us  knew  George,  and  he  took  the  advice  seriously,  worked 
with  us  closely. 

I  would  have  to  say,  though,  that  in  getting  these  people,  many  of 
whom  were  strong  critics  of  that  program,  he  took  a  chance.   I  remember  one 
friend  I  called  up  [John  Pierce]  who  had  claimed  this  wasn't  worth  doing, 
and  he  was  something  of  a  wit,  and  he  said,  "Well,  you  know,  I  always 
figured  that  something  not  worth  doing  isn't  worth  doing  well."  So  he 
wasn't  going  to  serve.   [laughter]   But  there  was  not  another  person  who 
wasn't  willing  to  serve.   And  I  think  it  made  a  difference. 

Coupled  with  this  friendship,  and  very  similar,  is  the  question  of  a 
sense  of  trust,  which  is  not  necessarily  based  on  friendship,  of  course. 
Friendship  is  only  one  aspect  that  helps,  but  a  sense  of  trust  that  really 
we  are  all  in  this  together,  a  feeling  that  we  basically  are  trying  to  make 
the  national  effort  a  success,  and  thereby  helping  the  people  who  are 
trying  to  carry  out  national  policy.  That  is  basically  what  we  are  all 
about.   We  are  all  in  the  same  boat--.   [tape  interruption] 

John  Wheeler  I  think  was  quite  right  in  saying  that  Project  137 
really  was  a  kind  of  birth,  or  pre -birth  pangs,  for  Jason.   Jason  is 
somewhat  modeled  on  that  project  and  did  grow  out  of  it.   I  have  looked 
over  some  of  the  records  so  that  we  could  be  straight  on  this.   I  know  how 
memories  fade,  including  certainly  my  own. 

It  was  in  the  summer  of  '59  I  had  a  letter  from  Marvin  Stern,  who  was 
an  applied  mathematician.   He  wrote  to  Gary  Norton  who  was  the  president  of 
IDA,  saying  that,  "During  the  past  several  months,  a  group  of  my  colleagues 
and  I  have  discussed  the  underlying  idea  which  generated  Project  137." 
Then  he  went  on  to  say  that  he  thought  it  really  needed  a  different  kind  of 
context  which  maybe  wouldn't  take  people  out  of  the  universities.   And  he 
quoted  Jim  Killian  in  a  letter  Jim  Killian  had  written  to  Marvin  Stern  or 
Wheeler,  and  [Eugene  Paul]  Wigner  a  little  earlier,  saying,  "We  do  need  to 
find  a  better  mechanism  or  mechanisms  to  permit  more  first  rate  scientists 
to  contribute  to  national  security,  and  to  do  so  in  an  environment  in  which 


675 


they  can  work  creatively  and  with  a  minimum  of  restrictions.   It  is  also 
important  that  there  be  continuous  and  systematic  exploration  of  science 
and  national  security,  free  exchange  of  ideas,  and  reporting  of  important 
scientific  developments  to  officers  and  agencies  of  the  government  dealing 
with  national  policy.   These  are  all  exceedingly  important  goals." 

Now,  that  was  the  initiation  of  Project  137.   As  I  say,  I  think  it 
was  Marvin  Stern  that  first  approached  IDA  saying,  "Well,  the  group  who  are 
my  friends  and  frequently  gather  at  Los  Alamos  really  ought  to  be  thinking 
about  being  connected  with  IDA  or  something  like  it."  They  were  thinking 
of  forming  a  company  to  do  consulting,  as  a  base  from  which  they  could  do 
consulting  and  work  on  national  problems,  but  of  course  that  was  very  much 
more  limited  and  would  have  restricted  them  and  this  was  what  Marvin  Stern 
and  many  others  argued. 

I  came  into  the  picture,  really,  September  1st.  I  had  been 
approached  to  go  down  to  Washington.   I  had  the  same  struggle  that  John 
Wheeler  did  as  to,  "Yes,  it  is  very  important,  but  to  leave  what  I  am 
doing" --the  laser  was  just  getting  hot  at  that  point- -"to  leave  what  I  am 
doing  and  to  go  down  to  Washington  full-time,  that  is  quite  an 
interruption."  But  I  was  persuaded  to  do  it  for  two  years,  and  I  went  down 
on  September  1,  and  tried  to  get  into  the  swing  of  things.   I  learned  about 
Marvin  Stern,  and  this  kind  of  possibility,  and  went  to  work  on  that  pretty 
fast. 

George  Sutton  was  then  head  of  ARPA.   Herb  [Herbert  Frank]  York  was 
his  boss.   Herb  York  had  been  brought  into  Washington  as  an  IDA  employee  to 
head  ARPA.   ARPA  had  no  personnel  initially,  so  they  got  IDA  to  hire  the 
people  and  get  the  thing  started.   Again,  there  was  the  possible  conflict 
between  the  outsiders  who  were  independent,  and  the  insiders  who  really 
knew  what  they  wanted,  and  eventually  ARPA  then  became  a  full-time 
government  service,  which  I  think  in  the  long  run  it  had  to  be. 
Temporarily,  it  was  under  the  people  who  were  hired  by  IDA  to  get  going 
quickly,  and  Herb  York  was  head  of  it.   Herb  had  moved  up  to  assistant 
secretary  for  R  &  D  at  that  point. 

George  [Harry]  Sutton,  whom  you  may  not  know  (George  was  a  propulsion 
man),  was  head  of  ARPA  at  that  point.   By  September  25,  this  was  a  letter 
he  wrote  to  me,  "Several  of  us  here  at  ARPA  have  had  a  chance  to  read  the 
draft  of  your  letter  to  Gary  Norton  explaining  your  proposal  on  a  modified 
Project  137.  We  are  all  agreed  that  it  is  a  very  good  project  to  use  the 
talents  of  capable  physicists  without  pulling  them  away  from  their  normal 
university  or  research  environments,  and  put  these  talents  to  work  on  basic 
research  proposals  of  strong  interest  to  the  Department  of  Defense." 

I  had  had  some  contacts  with  the  group  by  then.   Marvin  Stern  and  I 
went  out  to  Los  Alamos  and  talked  with  Murph  [Marvin  Leonard]  Goldberger, 
Ken  Watson,  Keith  [Allan]  Brueckner,  and  maybe  some  others.   They  were  sort 
of  the  organizing  committee,  plus  Murray  Gell-Mann--I  think  Murray  Cell- 


676 


Mann  wasn't  there  at  the  time.   And  Marvin  was  enormously  helpful.   He  was 
a  very  bright  guy- -I  haven't  seen  much  of  him  recently- -and  somewhat 
obstreperous.   I  remember  H.  P.  Robinson  in  some  of  our  discussions  with 
Marvin  saying,  "Look,  Marvin,  just  remember  1  was  a  boy  genius  once,  too!" 
[laughter] 

We  had  talked  things  over  pretty  thoroughly,  and  I  think  it  was 
really  kind  of  a  group  plan,  basically,  that  we  worked  out,  and  everybody 
was  involved.   But  we  had  some  trouble  getting  this  plan  over.   It  was  a 
golden  opportunity,  and  eventually  got  worked  out,  I  think,  well.   One 
thing  George  Sutton  felt,  "Well,  it  really  ought  to  report  to  ARPA;  it 
ought  to  be  a  part  of  ARPA  and  part  of  the  Pentagon,"  and  that  was  the 
right  way  to  run  it.   I  didn't  feel  that  that  would  be  the  appropriate 
context  and  John  Kincaid,  who  was  another  important  IDA  employee- -was  vice- 
chairman  of  ARPA- -differed  with  George  on  that  and  said  the  following: 
"This  alternative  would  lead  to  a  serious  restriction  on  access  for  the 
members  of  the  group  to  many  individuals  throughout  the  Department  of 
Defense."  Remember  ARPA  was  then  a  separate  service,  supposedly.   "This 
follows  because  members  of  the  Advanced  Research  Projects  Division  of  the 
Institute  for  Defense  Analyses  are  generally  presumed  to  speak  for  ARPA. 
This  would  inevitably  lead  to  restrictions  on  the  freedom  of  the  group  to 
see  people  from  whom  information  is  needed  on  the  freest  possible  basis." 
It,  of  course,  probably  also  would  have  somewhat  restricted  the  people  and 
made  a  different  administrative  setup. 

Now,  this  is  some  of  the  letter  which  I  had  written  to  try  to  outline 
the  proposal.   Here  is  how  some  of  it  went,  I  was  writing  to  Gary  Norton, 
the  president  of  IDA: 

"I  am  sure  you  are  well  aware  of  the  importance  of  close 
contact  on  the  part  of  outstanding  professional  persons  with 
national  affairs,  and  of  an  easy  flow  of  ideas  and  personnel 
between  governmental  organizations  and  intellectual  centers 
such  as  our  universities.   A  particular  need,  which  is  also 
fairly  widely  recognized,  is  good  contact  and  communication 
with  the  younger  established  scientists  who  are  in  their  most 
productive  period,  many  of  whom  have  not  yet  become  very 
familiar  with  problems  of  national  security,  as  has  the 
generation  which  was  active  in  such  affairs  during  World  War 
II." 

So,  there  is  the  documentary  evidence  saying  that  that  was  a  primary  reason 
for  trying  to  organize  this  group . 

"Many  in  this  group  are,  I  believe,  quite  ready  to 
undertake  serious  assignments  to  assist  national  security. 
However  a  satisfactory  mechanism  must  be  found  which  will 
provide  adequate  flexibility  and  a  minimum  of  restrictions,  so 
that  they  can  contribute  effectively  without  unnecessary 


677 

sacrifice  of  their  professional  careers.   Extensive  discussions 
were  held  at  Los  Alamos  with  firueckner,  Goldberger  and  Vatson, 
and  briefer  talks  with  [Norman]  Kroll,  [Conrad]  Longmire, 
[Francis]  Low,  and  [Sam]  Treiman.   Some  members  of  this  group 
have  already  spent  considerable  time  on  the  questions  involved, 
partly  as  a  sequel  to  Project  137,  and  some  resulting  ideas 
have  already  been  indicated  in  Stern's  letter  of  July  7  to  you. 

"Hence,  we  were  able  to  proceed  rather  far  in  outlining 
an  extensive  organization  and  method  of  work  which  seems  very 
promising,  and  on  which  there  is  general  agreement.   The 
proposal,  which  is  indicated  below,  cannot  fill  all  needs. 
However,  it  represents  a  start  which  in  itself  appears  to  be 
very  useful,  and  may  have  the  flexibility  and  potential  to 
grow. 

Now,  another  part  of  this  letter  talks  about  the  proposed  function  of  the 
group : 

"Broadly  speaking,  this  group  would  be  a  means  of  helping 
to  bring  the  academic  community  into  closer  contact  with 
pressing  national  problems  so  that  every  advantage  may  be  taken 
of  new  scientific  developments,  or  new  understanding,  and 
individuals  may,  where  appropriate,  direct  their  normal 
research  activities  towards  the  solution  of  these  problems.   It 
should  help  provide  skillful  and  imaginative  study  and  advice 
for  the  government  from  time  to  time,  stimulate  those  who  are 
already  in  government  service,  and  provide  a  somewhat  easier 
flow  of  ideas  and  personnel  between  government  and  the  academic 
community. 

"The  group  would  be  available  for  advice  and  work  on  some 
specific  problems  which  arise  in  ARPA,  WSEG,  or  in  branches  of 
the  Defense  Department.   However,  it  should  not  be  regarded 
primarily  as  a  service  bureau  for  these  organizations.   Rather, 
its  primary  task  would  be  to  undertake  specific  study  and 
investigation  in  areas  of  national  security  where  such  effort 
appears  most  rewarding.  This  process  would  itself  probably 
establish  effective  communication  and  stimulation  between 
government  organizations,  and  important  parts  of  the  academic 
community. 

Now  here  is  what  is  said  about  future  possibilities: 

"This  program  may  be  considered  experimental,  and  in  any 
case  should  have  flexibility  to  develop  in  directions  which 
experience  finds  are  possible.   The  ability  and  enthusiasm  of 
the  group  should  be  adequate  assurance  of  its  success.   It 
seems  also  to  have  potentiality  for  very  striking  success  and 


678 


effectiveness.   If  so,  the  group  may  well  expand  into  what 
might  be  described  as  an  academy  for  national  problems, 
including  additional  divisions  and  steering  committees  in 
various  disciplines  other  than  the  physical  sciences,  which 
would  be  an  important  source  of  strength  to  our  national  effort 
in  a  variety  of  fields. 

"But  in  any  case,  those  involved  in  the  present 
discussions  believe  that  the  purposes  of  the  organization  can 
be  best  served  by  very  careful  selection  of  personnel  on  the 
basis  of  proven  ability  and  hence  by  careful  and  not  too  rapid 
extension. " 

Now,  the  people  being  suggested  in  this  letter  —  and  let  me  say  the 
particular  people  varied  a  little  bit,  some  people  said  maybe  they  would  do 
it  and  maybe  they  wouldn't,  and  a  few  came  in  just  a  day  or  two  later  than 
this  letter,  and  so  on- -so  this  was  not  a  final  list,  but  this  was  a  list 
as  of  that  time:   Keith  Brueckner,  Chandrasekhar ,  Freeman  [John]  Dyson,  Val 
Fitch,  Ed  Freiman,  Dick  Garwin,  Murray  Gell-Mann,  Don  [Donald]  Glaser, 
Murph  Goldberger,  Bob  Corner- -the  one  physical  chemist  in  the  group  —  Bob 
Karplus ,  Norman  Kroll,  Leon  [Max]  Lederman,  Bob  LeLevier,  Hal  [Harold 
Warren]  Lewis,  [Morton]  Leonard  Lieberman,  Conrad  Longmire,  Francis  Lowe, 
Elliot  [Waters]  Montroll,  Arnold  Nordsieck,  Eugene  [Newman]  Parker,  Alan 
[W. ]  Peterson,  Fred  Reines,  Marshall  [N.]  Rosenbluth,  Sam  Treiman,  Ken 
Watson. 

It  is  also  stated  in  this  letter  that  Professors  Bethe,  Teller, 
Wigner,  and  Wheeler  have  agreed  to  give  assistance  to  the  group  in  some 
capacity  if  its  plans  mature.  That  was  very  important.   That  was  a  senior 
group,  you  see,  who  were  going  to  help  guide  and  sort  of  guarantee  that  the 
thing  would  be  set  up  well.   They  were  people  of  experience,  and  I  think 
that  was  important  to  the  planning,  and  the  Pentagon.   And  these  people  did 
give  us  very  substantial  advice- -these  four  senior  statesmen  of  the  time. 

"Additions  may  be  made  to  bring  in  some  physical  chemists 
or  other  viewpoints  in  the  general  area  of  the  physical 
sciences.   Some  effort  has  been  made  to  obtain  good 
geographical  distribution  and  wide  contacts  of  the  group,  and 
at  the  same  time  to  have  as  many  as  two  members  in  the  same 
city  who  can  easily  work  and  discuss  confidential  matters 
together. " 

So  that  was  all  part  of  the  original  plan.   Now  let  me  say,  also,  that  in 
that  line-up  of  personnel,  one  very  important  person  was  Dave  Katcher  who 
was  then  an  employee  of  IDA,  who  came  in  to  help  us  and  was  full  of  ideas 
and  good  cheer  and  knew  how  to  do  things.   I  would  say  he  was  almost  as 
good  as  Don  Levine .   He  was  just  an  enormous  help. 


679 


Of  course,  the  whole  thing  really  depended  on  the  excellence  of  that 
group  of  people.   That  was  the  key  thing,  and  that  was  obvious  to 
everybody.   Exactly  what  organization  might  be  formed  was  somewhat 
variable,  but  if  those  people  were  interested  in  doing  something,  that  was 
terribly  important.   I  remember  very  well  one  of  our  senior  advisors,  as  we 
met  with  some  of  the  Jason  group,  telling  the  secretary  of  defense,  "See, 
across  the  room  there  is  the  best  mathematical  physics  mind  in  the 
country."  That  had  an  impact.   I  won't  say  who  it  was;  that  could  apply  to 
a  variety  of  people.   [laughter]  But  that  was  the  kind  of  quality  that  was 
there,  and  which  really  made  the  difference,  and  that  is  I  think  what  we 
have  to  keep  striving  for. 

There  were  other  happy  circumstances.  Of  course  IDA  had  an  important 
role  at  that  time  within  the  Pentagon  and  advising  the  Pentagon.   Herb  York 
was  assistant  secretary  for  R  &  D  and  very  friendly.   Tom  [Thomas  S.]  Gates 
was  then  secretary  of  defense,  and  he  was  a  close  personal  friend  of  Gary 
Norton- -they  had  worked  together  in  the  Navy  sometime  in  the  past- -and  Tom 
Gates  was  very  friendly.   I  have  a  letter  here  which  discusses  a  luncheon 
that  we  all  had  with  Herb  York  and  George  Kistiakowsky,  who  was,  of  course, 
advisor  to  the  president,  and  he  was  interested  and  helpful.   Keith  Glennan 
was  then  head  of  NASA.   We  all  thought  that  this  really,  while  it  would  be 
sponsored  by  the  Defense  Department,  it  should  not  be  narrowly  defined,  and 
we  were  very  clear  with  the  Defense  Department  about  that.   It  should  not 
be  narrowly  defined,  there  were  problems  in  the  national  interest- -and 
certainly  the  space  program  is  one  of  those- -and  Keith  Glennan  was  very 
interested.   And  then  there  was  the  three  service  secretaries. 

Veil,  fortunately,  the  administration  was  persuaded.   I  have  a  letter 
here,  late  September,  from  Roy  Johnson.  Roy  Johnson  was  the  titular  head 
of  ARPA.   You  probably  don't  know  much  about  him.   He  was  appointed  by  Neil 
McElroy.   He  was  a  General  Electric  appliance  salesman.   They  had  to  find 
somebody  who  was  an  administrator  and  knew  something  about  technology  who 
could  fit  in  with  that,  and  suddenly  they  needed  somebody  to  run  ARPA,  and 
he  was  an  administrator.   Herb  York  was,  of  course,  the  scientist  who  made 
most  of  the  technical  decisions.   We  rarely  saw  Roy  Johnson,  but  he  was 
very  insistent  that  ARPA  was  equal  in  importance  to  all  of  the  other 
services,  and  he  maintained  that  position  very  strongly  as  long  as  he  was 
there.   He  was,  late  that  year,  replaced  by  General  [Austin  Wortham]  Betts, 
who  then  acceded  to  some  modification  in  the  official  position  of  ARPA 
without  any  real  change  in  its  function. 

But  this  letter  from  Roy  Johnson  talks  about  my  letter  and  says,  "In 
the  new  version,  various  capable  research  professors  and  universities  and 
technical  institutes  will  devote  a  portion  of  their  time  to  work  on 
specific  research  problems  of  interest  to  the  Department  of  Defense.   In 
this  unique  manner,  they  can  contribute  to  our  efforts,  yet  they  will  not 
be  dislocated  from  their  laboratory  or  college  environment."  You  see  what 
his  understanding  of  universities  was,  there  weren't  many  of  these  people 
from  colleges.   But  nevertheless,  he  was  a  broad-scale  intelligent  person, 


680 


and  in  this  letter  he  talks  about  this  as  being  "a  modification  of  last 
year's  effort,"  which  was  the  Project  137,  so  it  is  very  clear  that  it  was 
a  follow- on  from  that. 

And  here  is  a  letter  from  General  Betts.  Now  we  had  worked  things 
out,  and  General  Betts  was  defining  for  the  Defense  Department  what  the  job 
was.   "The  new  group  would  undertake  a  program  to  study  basic  defense 
research  problems,  one.   Number  two,  make  contributions  of  a  conceptual 
nature  towards  solutions  of  DOD  technical  problems.   Three,  identify  basic 
problems  which  are  vital  to  national  defense,  and  which  are  not  now 
receiving  adequate  attention  in  the  scientific  community.   And  four,  advise 
the  secretary  of  defense,  through  the  director  of  the  Advanced  Research 
Projects  Agency  and  the  director  of  Defense  Research  and  Engineering,  of 
scientific  developments  on  which  projects  might  be  initiated  and  which 
would  enhance  national  security.   It  is  understood  this  project  will 
utilize  a  group  of  the  country's  best  young  scientists,  thus  increasing  the 
defense  capability  in  our  basic  science  effort.   It  is  anticipated  that 
encouragement  will  be  given  to  analytical  studies  and  theoretical 
deliberations  and  that  minimal  expenditures  will  be  made  for  computers, 
assistants,  and  actual  experimental  research  work  on  this  program."  They 
weren't  undertaking  those  kinds  of  expenses,  you  see,  and  we  tried  to 
reassure  them  on  this . 

"The  Advanced  Research  Projects  Agency  is  prepared  to  support  this 
effort  by  making  available  information  within  the  DOD." 

Now  that  says  a  whale  of  a  lot  more  than  it  might  initially  appear  to 
say.   That  was  a  very  important  concession  they  made,  to  give  the  group  a 
very  broad-scale  clearance  to  work  with  any  service  and,  in  principle,  look 
into  anything.   "The  Secretary  of  Defense  and  the  director  of  defense 
research  and  engineering  have  expressed  their  keen  interest  in  the  concept 
of  the  group,  and  have  specifically  directed  the  project  be  pursued 
vigorously."  That  was  Betts 's  memorandum  dated,  I  notice,  January  1,  1960. 
I  wonder  [chuckles]  about  that  date,  but  it  is  surely  approximately  right. 

Finally,  I  got  a  telephone  message,  taken  by  my  secretary  at  the 
time,  saying  the  following  (this  is  on  January  4):  "Mr.  Button's 
secretary"- -remember  Sutton  was  the  IDA  employee  who  was  then  the 
scientific  director  of  ARPA--"Mr.  Button's  secretary  phoned  to  say  that 
they  had  heard  from  Sam  Clements  of  R&E  by  way  of  a  note  to  General  Betz 
and  Mr.  Sutton.   Mr.  Clements  said  that  the  revised  ARPA  directive  was 
approved  by  Mr.  Gates  [who  was  secretary  of  defense]  as  drafted.  Dr.  York 
okayed  the  project  assignment  on  the  IDA  proposition,  and  therefore  IDA  can 
go  ahead  with  it. " 

The  project  assignment  was  not  Jason,  it  was  Sunrise- -this  was 
Project  Sunrise.   That  was  George  Button's  name,  and  it  sounded  kind  of 
glorious,  but  I  think  nobody  really  liked  it  [laughter].   Now  we  come  to 
the  history  of  the  name.   Murph  and  everybody  felt,  "That  doesn't  really 


681 


fit  us;  we  just  don't  feel  that  is  the  right  kind  of  name."  And  I  said, 
"Well,  why  don't  you  think  about  it  and  say  what  name  you  would  like." 

Now,  the  steering  committee,  which  I  have  failed  to  mention 
specifically,  was  also  chosen.   The  steering  committee  was  Murph ,  Ken 
Watson,  Murray  Cell -Mann,  and  Keith  Brueckner.   I  asked  them  who  should  be 
head  of  this.   "Oh,"  they  said,  "Murph.   In  our  planning,  Murph  was  always 
going  to  be  president  of  the  company.   Murph  is  just  the  natural  president 
type,  so  Murph's  the  guy."  I  said,  "Well,  okay,  if  that  is  what  you  want, 
that's  fine."   So  Murph  was  chairman,  and  this  was  the  steering  committee. 
I  told  the  steering  committee,  "Well,  choose  what  name  you  like."  Murph 
came  back  to  me  a  while  later  and  said,  "Well,  you  know,  we've  been  talking 
about  it,  and  what  about  Jason?"   I  said,  "That's  fine.   Where  did  that 
come  from?"   He  said,  "As  a  matter  of  fact,  Mildred  suggested  it,  and 
everybody  finds  it  okay."   I  said,  "That's  fine.   After  all,  if  you  are 
launching  a  new  enterprise,  how  could  you  have  it  christened  better  than  by 
the  wife  of  the  chairman?"   [chuckles]   So  that  is  how  the  name  came  about. 
We  were  not  yet  bright  enough  to  wake  up  to  its  calendric  meaning  at  that 
time.   [laughter]   That  occurred  later. 

Now,  there  were  other  matters  which  continued  to  require  some  work  to 
clear  up.   I  never  told  Jason  people  this,  but  it  was  a  terribly  difficult 
job  getting  all  of  these  bumptious  young  scientists  cleared.   I  hope  they 
didn't  know  it  [laughs],  but  we  had  to  work  terribly  hard  getting  all  of 
these  people  cleared.   They  were  outspoken  people,  young  people  who  were 
chosen  on  their  scientific  ability,  not  on  their  conservatism  and 
reliability- -that  wasn't  the  idea.   I  think  the  Pentagon  really  reached  out 
and  took  a  chance,  the  kind  of  chance  we  should  take  on  occasion,  in 
saying,  "These  are  outstanding  people.   We  must  pull  our  society  together 
and--" 

[end  of  tape] 


Transcribed  by  Kian  Sandjideh

January  1994 
Interviews  on  the  History  of  The  University  of  California 

Documenting  the  history  of  the  University  of  California  has  been  a 
responsibility  of  the  Regional  Oral  History  Office  since  the  Office  was 
established  in  1954.  Oral  history  memoirs  with  University-related 
persons  are  listed  below.  They  have  been  underwritten  by  the  UC  Berkeley 
Foundation,  the  Chancellor's  Office,  University  departments,  or  by 
extramural  funding  for  special  projects.  The  oral  histories,  both  tapes 
and  transcripts,  are  open  to  scholarly  use  in  The  Bancroft  Library. 
Bound,  indexed  copies  of  the  transcripts  are  available  at  cost  to 
manuscript  libraries. 

Adams,  Frank,  "Irrigation,  Reclamation,  and  Water  Administration,"  1956. 

Amerine,  Maynard  A.,  "The  University  of  California  and  the  State's  Wine 
Industry,"  1971.   [UC  Davis] 

Amerine,  Maynard  A.,  "Wine  Bibliographies  and  Taste  Perception  Studies," 
1988.   [UC  Davis]

University  of  California,"  1978.   Two  volumes.

MULTI- INTERVIEWEE  PROJECTS 

"Blake  Estate  Oral  History  Project."  1988. 

Architects  landscape  architects,  gardeners,  presidents  of  UC 
document  the  history  of  the  UC  presidential  residence.   Includes 
interviews  with  Mai  Arbegast,  Igor  Blake,  Ron  and  Myra  Brocchini, 
Toichi  Domoto,  Eliot  Evans,  Tony  Hail,  Linda  Haymaker,  Charles 
Hitch,  Flo  Holmes,  Clark  and  Kay  Kerr,  Gerry  Scott,  George  and 
Helena  Thacher,  Walter  Vodden,  and  Norma  Wilier. 

"Centennial  History  Project,  1954-1960," 

Includes  interviews  with  George  P.  Adams,  Anson  Stiles  Blake,  Walter 
C.  Blasdale,  Joel  H.  Hildebrand,  Samuel  J.  Holmes,  Alfred  L. 
Kroeber,  Ivan  M.  Linforth,  George  D.  Louderback,  Agnes  Fay  Morgan, 
and  William  Popper.   [Bancroft  Library  use  only] 

"Thomas  D.  Church,  Landscape  Architect,"  two  volumes,  1978. 

Volume  I:   Includes  interviews  with  Theodore  Bernardi,  Lucy  Butler, 
June  Meehan  Campbell,  Louis  De  Monte,  Walter  Doty,  Donn  Emmons , 
Floyd  Gerow,  Harriet  Henderson,  Joseph  Howland,  Ruth  Jaffe,  Burton 
Litton,  Germano  Milano,  Miriam  Pierce,  George  Rockrise,  Robert 
Royston,  Geraldine  Knight  Scott,  Roger  Sturtevant,  Francis  Violich, 
and  Harold  Watkin. 

Volume  II:   Includes  interviews  with  Maggie  Baylis,  Elizabeth  Roberts 
Church,  Robert  Glasner,  Grace  Hall,  Lawrence  Halprin,  Proctor 
Mellquist,  Everitt  Miller,  Harry  Sanders,  Lou  Schenone,  Jack 
Stafford,  Goodwin  Steinberg,  and  Jack  Wagstaff . 

"Dental  History  Project,  University  of  California,  San  Francisco,"  1969. 
Includes  interviews  with  Dickson  Bell,  Reuben  L.  Blake,  Willard  C. 
Fleming,  George  A.  Hughes,  Leland  D.  Jones,  George  F.  McGee ,  C.E. 
Rutledge,  William  B.  Ryder,  Jr.,  Herbert  J.  Samuels,  Joseph  Sciutto, 
William  S.  Smith,  Harvey  Stallard,  George  E.  Steninger,  and  Abraham 
W.  Ward.   [Bancroft  Library  use  only] 

"Julia  Morgan  Architectural  History  Project,"  Two  volumes,  1976. 

Volume  I:   "The  Work  of  Walter  Steilberg  and  Julia  Morgan,  and  the 
Department  of  Architecture,  UCB,  1904-1954." 

Includes  interviews  with  Walter  T.  Steilberg,  Robert  Ratcliff , 
Evelyn  Paine  Ratcliff,  Norman  L.  Jensen,  John  E.  Wagstaff,  George  C. 
Hodges,  Edward  B.  Hussey,  and  Warren  Charles  Perry. 

Volume  II:   "Julia  Morgan,  Her  Office,  and  a  House." 

Includes  interviews  with  Mary  Grace  Barron,  Kirk  0.  Rowlands,  Norma 
Wilier,  Quintilla  Williams,  Catherine  Freeman  Nimitz,  Polly  Lawrence 
McNaught,  Hettie  Belle  Marcus,  Bjarne  Dahl,  Bjarne  Dahl ,  Jr.,  Morgan 
North,  Dorothy  Worms er  Coblentz,  and  Flora  d'llle  North. 

"The  Prytaneans :  An  Oral  History  of  the  Prytanean  Society  and  its 
Members."   [Order  from  Prytanean  Society] 

Volume  I:    "1901-1920,"  1970. 

Volume  II:    "1921-1930,"  1977. 

Volume  III:   "1931-1935,"  1990. 


Robert  Gordon  Sproul  Oral  History  Project.*  Two  volumes,  1986. 

Includes  interviews  with  Horace  Albright,  Stuart  LeRoy  Anderson, 
Rather ine  Bradley,  Dyke  Brown,  Natalie  Cohen,  Paul  A.  Dodd,  May 
Dornin,  Richard  E.  Erickson,  Walter  S.  Frederick,  David  P.  Gardner, 
Vernon  Goodin,  Marion  Sproul  Goodin,  Louis  Heilbron,  Clark  Kerr, 
Adrian  Kragen,  Robert  S.  Johnson,  Mary  Bluaer  Lawrence,  Donald

Suzanne  Bassett  Riess 

Grew  up  in  Bucks  County,  Pennsylvania.   Graduated  from 
Goucher  College,  B.A.  in  English,  1957. 
Post-graduate  work,  University  of  London  and  the 
University  of  California,  Berkeley,  in  English  and 
history  of  art. 

Feature  writing  and  assistant  woman's  page  editor, 
Globe-Times .  Bethlehem,  Pennsylvania. 
Volunteer  work  on  starting  a  new  Berkeley  newspaper. 
Natural  science  decent  at  the  Oakland  Museum. 
Free-lance  Photographer. 

Editor  in  the  Regional  Oral  History  Office  since  1960, 
interviewing  in  the  fields  of  art,  environmental 
design,  social  and  cultural  hiitory,  horticulture, 
journalism,  photography,  Berkeley  and  University 
history.
